title: Penn State softball falls to No. 3 Nebraska in late-inning collapse
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
In Penn State’s first game of Saturday's doubleheader, No.3 Nebraska took the first win of the day. The blue and white took on the Cornhuskers for their second game of the series Saturday afternoon.

The Nittany Lions got ahead of No.3 Nebraska but were unable to hold the lead falling 10-4.

Head Coach Matt Campbell started things off with the first pitch, his first ceremonial pitch so far in his time in Happy Valley.

Junior right hander Bridget Nemeth got the start in the circle for the blue and white. Starting off strong, Nemeth had a quick inning getting her first two strikeouts of the game against. With one of her strikeouts being senior utility Jordy Frahm.

Alexis Jensen, the freshman left hander, started on the mound for Nebraska.

Freshman outfielder Breanna Hanik singled up the middle on her first pitch of the game. Junior infielder Kaylie Walters attempted a bunt but was out, with Hanik moving up to second.

A fielding error from the Cornhuskers’ defense gave freshman infielder Allison Oneacre first base. That was all for the Nittany Lion offense as they left Hanik and Oneacre stranded on base.

Nemeth surrendered her first walk of the game in the top of the second, but was able to have another quick inning as she got her third strikeout of the game to keep the scoreboard clean, 0-0 going in the bottom of the second.

Jensen gave up a walk, giving sophomore catcher Macy Chamberlin first base, but that was all for the Penn State offense as Jensen was able to secure three strikeouts in the bottom of the second.

A walk by Nemeth gave senior infielder Lauren Camenzind first base, and Frahm was able to reach first off a defensive error from the blue and white. Another walk got three Nebraska runners on base with only one out in the top of the third.

Oneacre was able to secure the second out of the inning with a pop up, but a wild pitch brought Camenzind home to tally the first run of the game. A fly out ended the inning and the Cornhuskers momentum.

Nebraska led 1-0 going into the bottom of the third, where the top of the Penn State order was due. Hanik fouled out, and on Walters first pitch at the plate she hit a double that just grasped the foul line.

Oneacre launched the ball out of Beard Field, grabbing the lead with a two run homer. Penn State led 2-1 in the bottom of the third with just one out.

Junior utility Brooke Klosowicz tried to get the second home run of the game, but was unsuccessful as a Nebraska outfielder was able to catch the pop up.

Nemeth made an appearance in the batters box and was able to get a hit, but the Huskers defense was able to get the final out of the inning. The blue and white led 2-1 entering the fourth.

Two runners were left on base for Nebraska as Nemeth kept the board clean by securing two more strikeouts.

Chamberlain started things off with a single down the left side. Freshman Kali Kawowski entered the game as a pitch runner, and attempted to steal second, but the Huskers defense was able to catch her attempt..

Freshman infielder Gabby Gradishar won the battle against Jensen, advancing to first after being walked. Junior outfielder Frankee Flesher continued the momentum for the blue and white with a single through the right. Gradishar at second and Flesher at first.

Hanik stepped into the batters box and hit a single to left fielding bringing in Gradishar to increase the lead to 3-1. Junior Jordan George entered the game but was unsuccessful, striking out and leaving two Nittany Lions on base.

The Penn State outfield had a 1,2,3 inning as the defense stepped in the top of the fifth.

With two outs on the bottom of the fifth, Junior infielder Michela Barbanente crushed the ball out of the park for a solo home run. Extending the blue and white’s lead 4-1. That was all for the Penn State offense in the bottom of the fifth.

The Nebraska offense woke up in the top of the sixth, getting bases loaded with just one out, and a single from Kacie Hoffman got two Huskers home. Despite the two runs, the blue and white still lead 4-3 in the sixth.

Freshman right hander Mackenize Duncan was called up from the bullpen to close out the inning. Duncan had two runners on with only one out, and surrendered her first walk of the game with bases loaded.

Frahm continued the momentum with a single to right field, getting another Husker home to tie the game at 4-4.

A double got two more runners home, and Nebraska took the lead with only one out. Junior right-hander McKenna Young entered the game in relief for the Nittany Lions.

Penn State was able to close out the top of the sixth, but another run was scored for the Huskers as they took the lead 7-4 after scoring six runs in the sixth.

Frahm took over the circle in the bottom of the sixth. Getting her first strikeout against Sophomore Natalie Lieto. Gradishar was unsuccessful, getting struck out swinging and Flesher was grounded out. That was all for the blue and white in the bottom of the sixth.

Nebraska followed through with more momentum going into the seventh as another run was scored, extending their lead to 8-4. Camenzind came out for more as she launched a homer to left field, getting two more runs scored.

The blue and white were able to close out the top of the seventh, the Huskers scored three extending their lead 10-4.

In the bottom of the seventh the Penn State order was due, Hanik grounded out. Walters was struck out, and Oneacre looked to keep things alive with a single up the middle before Klosowicz grounded out.

Nebraska took the win 10-4.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State track and field concludes outdoor regular season at Larry Ellis Invitational
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State wrapped up another record-breaking outdoor season with another weekend with more big performances.

The Nittany Lions competed at the Larry Ellis Invitational at Princeton on Friday and Saturday, earning 14 personal bests and five top-10 finishes in school history.

Friday

Freshman Matthew Sarnowski got things going on Friday in the men’s discus throw, where he earned a personal best for third place, throwing 51.55 meters.

Penn State earned its first shakeup in the record books in the women’s hammer throw, as senior Alex Pancoast threw a nearly five-meter personal best. Pancoast finished third with a throw of 60.09 meters — the sixth-best in Penn State history.

In the first event on the track, freshman Boh Ritchie set the No. 5 mark in school history in the women’s 800-meters, running 2:03.51 for third place.

In the men’s 800m, junior Kaleb Nastari finished fourth in 1:48.37, followed by teammate Carter Fitzgerald in fifth in 1:48.47.

Sophomore Kyle Moore continued to experience a breakout season in the men’s long jump, recording a personal best distance of 7.78 meters, good enough for second place at the meet and seventh-best in school history. Joe White finished just behind in fourth, jumping 7.21 meters for a season best.

Senior Lea Hatcher led five Nittany Lions in the women’s 1,500-meters, finishing third with a season-best 4:16.84. Katie Dallas finished in fifth with a personal best 4:19.99.

Despite multiple top-10 marks in school history earlier in the invitational, Allon Clay earned the first school record of the day in the Mike Brady Elite Men’s 1,500m. The Kanagawa, Japan, native ran 3:37.84 to win the race, breaking Brannon Kidder’s previous mark from 2014.

Senior Ryan Watson finished in third place in the 1,500m, running 3:39.38 to improve his top-10 mark to the sixth-best in school history.

In the men’s 3,000-meter steeplechase, senior Justin Healey capped off the final personal best on the day, running 8:52.09 for fifth place.

Saturday

Sophomore Hannah Riolo kicked things off on Saturday in the women’s high jump, where she earned a second-place finish with 1.79 meters.

The Nittany Lions got right back to their successful ways early in the competition as freshman Skye-Petrie Cameron threw the fifth-best shot put in Penn State history. Petrie-Cameron finished second in the event, throwing for 16.68 meters.

Junior Matt Dematteo hit the track for the men’s 400-meter hurdles, where he finished fourth overall in 52.21 seconds.

Sophomore Alexandria North wrapped up the day in the women’s 200-meters, where she finished second with a time of 24.22 seconds.

Up next

The Nittany Lions will compete at the Big Ten Championships in Lincoln, Nebraska, from May 15 to May 17.

MORE TRACK AND FIELD COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's basketball earns commitment from incoming freshman Ashlee Brown
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State's squad grows strong to seven players.

Incoming freshman Ashlee Brown committed to the Lady Lions on Saturday, marking the seventh player to commit under coach Tanisha Wright.

Brown is a 5-foot-9 guard from Example Academy Girls Prep School and was named a McDonald's All-American nominee in January.

The Iowa native is the second incoming freshman on Penn State's squad, joining 4-star Kamrah Banks.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball loses close series opener to Minnesota
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
After suffering another sweep last weekend to No. 19 Oregon, Penn State is still searching for its first series win of the season.

With the regular season winding down and their postseason chances dwindling, the Nittany Lions started a pivotal conference series at home against Minnesota on Saturday afternoon.

Penn State battled with Minnesota throughout the close series opener, but the Golden Gophers took the lead early and didn’t look back, winning 5-4 over the Nittany Lions.

The blue and white entered the contest second to last in the Big Ten at 12-29, while the Golden Gophers entered Happy Valley on a five-game winning streak and possessed a 27-17 record.

Coach Mike Gambino’s squad holds the worst ERA in the conference at 7.87, so limiting Minnesota’s bats was key to a series opener victory. However, the Golden Gophers struck first in the opening frame against Penn State’s ace Colin Fitzgerald.

The senior walked leadoff Jack Spanier on four pitches and then Davis Hamilton knocked an infield single to second. It didn’t take long for Minnesota to capitalize with two runners on and no outs as Weber Neels did damage. Neels launched a three-run blast to right center and the Golden Gophers were quickly up 3-0.

Fitzgerald soon found him in another jam shortly after Neels cleared the bases. Charlie Sutherland poked a single over to shortstop and Ty Allen followed by smashing a double into the left center gap. With runners in scoring position, Easton Richter drew a long walk to load the bases.

Though Minnesota had a golden opportunity to blow the game wide open, the pressure didn’t affect Fitzgerald. The Lititz, Pennsylvania, native mowed down three straight Golden Gophers on strikeouts. Ethan Cole, Jack Bello and Jack Mosh all struck out swinging to stop the bleeding as Fitzgerald escaped the first with only three runs plating.

Right-hander Isaac Morton swiftly retired the Nittany Lions in their half of the first, but Fitzgerald faced more pressure in the second. Hamilton drew a one-out walk and then stole second. Fitzgerald then threw a wild pitch with two outs and Hamilton advanced into third. Despite Fitzgerald walking Sutherland, he retired the next Golden Gophers and escaped the inning without any runs scored.

The Nittany Lions broke through later in the second frame. Morton lost control and hit catcher Avery Smith, but struck out right fielder Jesse Jaconski for the second out. However, center fielder Maddox McDonald delivered and tugged an RBI triple to the backstop in right to bring Penn State closer 3-1. Yet, Penn State couldn’t close the distance any further as shortstop Preston Yaucher struck out to end the rally.

Though Minnesota quickly extended its lead back to three in the third. Richter knocked a leadoff single to right and then Fitzgerald hit Bello a few batters later. Mosh singled to first and plated Richter to extend the Golden Gophers’ lead 4-1.

Despite Morton cruising through the blue and white’s lineup, right hander Mason Horwat provided a few quality innings of relief. The junior surrendered just two hits and totaled three strikeouts in his two scoreless frames.

After a few shutdown innings, Penn State cut into the three-run hole in the fifth. Yaucher grounded a routine out to Spanier at shortstop, but he fumbled the ball. Davis then drew a five-pitch walk and designated hitter Michael Anderson extended the rally with a single into center. Second baseman Bryce Molinaro lifted a sacrifice fly to right and scored Yaucher to cut Minnesota’s lead in half 4-2.

Bar a few base knocks in the top of the sixth, right-hander Mason Butash delivered a scoreless frame and the Nittany Lions inched closer. Right-hander Adam Urban retired the first two batters, but Jaconski started a rally and knocked a single through the left side.

A few batters later, Yaucher plated Jaconski with a single through the right side to bring the score to 4-3. Yet, the Golden Gophers took the run right back in the seventh with a leadoff double by Richter and Bello scored him off a sacrifice fly to put the Nittany Lions back down two runs, 5-3.

The back-and-forth swing continued into the bottom half of the seventh. Molinaro powered a solo shot to right center and brought Penn State back within one, 5-4.

Both squads were shut down the last few frames of the game as the Nittany Lions couldn’t mount the complete comeback and fell in the series opener.

Up Next

Penn State will face Minnesota for the second game of Saturday’s doubleheader approximately 35 minutes after the conclusion of the series opener.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: No. 15 Penn State men’s lacrosse takes down No. 7 Johns Hopkins to secure Big Ten championship
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
The last time Penn State played for a Big Ten championship, it was flat-out embarrassed, falling to Michigan 16-4 in Columbus, Ohio.

In their last meeting with Johns Hopkins, the Nittany Lions fumbled a five-goal lead in the third quarter by allowing seven consecutive goals, ultimately being defeated 11-10.

None of that history mattered on Saturday, though. The coming of the weekend brought with it a clean slate and a chance for the Nittany Lions to claim their first conference title since 2019, when they outlasted the Blue Jays in an 18-17 barn burner.

The blue and white bucked the trends, delivering an all-around offensive performance to take down Johns Hopkins, securing the championship with a 16-8 win.

Sloppy passing on the game’s opening possession saw the Blue Jays throw the ball out of bounds. Penn State capitalized, as Hunter Aquino caught a pass and cut between Johns Hopkins defenders before firing a shot past goaltender Oran Gelinas.

Hunter Chauvette, who posted a hat trick in the regular-season meeting, got the Blue Jays on the board with a strong cut and powerful sidearm shot into the Nittany Lions’ net.

With the Penn State midfield group battling injury, Ryan DiRocco saw the most significant action of his season. After the Chauvette goal, he notched just his second score of the year on a powerful wind-up shot from Gelinas’ right side.

A Jameson Smith goal not long after sent the game into a media timeout with the score knotted at 2-2. Johns Hopkins got another late goal in the quarter from Chuck Rawson to take their first lead of the contest, which they held until the first break.

The score was leveled with 12:18 to go until halftime, when Liam Matthews cut to catch a pass from Luke Walstrum and flicked the ball into the nylon. It wouldn’t stay that way for long, as Jimmy Ayers worked past his pole and dove on the Penn State crease to dunk one on the Nittany Lions’ goaltender Preston Hawkins.

After a huge check on the ride, Aquino won back possession. Penn State got a big goal when Chase Robertson threw a big hitch and bounced one past Gelinas to even the score at 4-4. Less than a minute later, Kyle Lehman notched his first goal of the tournament to put the blue and white into the lead.

As time bled off the shot clock, Matthews took a dodge on his defender and took a shot as he fell to the turf, the ball finding its way into the cage as the buzzer sounded. Penn State took a 6-4 lead into the half.

Matt Collison, the 2026 Big Ten Offensive Player of the Year, made his presence felt after a slow start with a quick goal to start the second half. After the goal, coach Jeff Tambroni argued with the referee, looking for a penalty that would’ve nullified the Collison score.

The strong opening to the half continued, as Chauvette shot between Peri DiBartolomeo and Alex Ross to sink one past Hawkins and level the score at 6-6.

After multiple Penn State shots found the pipes, Walstrum cut up the alley from behind the net and faked out Gelinas, finishing in close to regain the Nittany Lion lead.

Andrew Beard notched five points in the blue and white’s regular-season finale against Rutgers at SHI Stadium, and scored a highlight-reel goal to stretch the lead to two, stutter-stepping and driving towards the crease before tucking a close shot into the nylon.

Jack Iannantuono made it a four-goal run and three-goal lead for Penn State as he dove in front of Gelinas and flicked it in for the score. The newly-named Big Ten Freshman of the Year was shaken up on the play and headed to the bench after the score.

A Chase Robertson shot seemed to find the underside of the crossbar, but wasn’t ruled a goal on the field. Tambroni threw the red flag to challenge the play, and contrary to a few notable examples in recent history, the replay went Penn State’s way and was called a goal.

Aquino got his fourth point of the game with a slick shot into the top corner of the net, matching his jersey number with goal number 23 in his injury-marred sophomore campaign.

The Blue Jays finally ended a six-goal Penn State run with a shot from Chauvette that slipped under Hawkins to find the net.

Soon after the Chauvette goal, Aquino came up injured and hopped to the sideline without putting weight on his sleeve-covered right leg, assumedly the one responsible for his absence in the regular season.

Michael Faraone joined in on the scoring, firing a low shot into the net on the man-up opportunity.

Lehman got his second soon after a face dodge led to a strong opportunity to score on Gelinas, and got his hat trick when the goaltender couldn’t contain a rebound. The Ambler, Pennsylvania native scooped the loose ball and lobbed it into the open net to bring the Penn State advantage to 14-7.

John Jude Considine ripped a shot into the top-right corner of the net to deliver his first goal of the game and further extend the Nittany Lion lead.

Chauvette scored a rocket to cut the lead to seven. Tambroni emptied his bench, and Lindenwood transfer Wes Scurci worked his defender before firing a perfectly-placed shot into the top-left corner of the net, bringing the score to 16-8. The score would hold until the final buzzer sounded.

Seconds bled off the clock, and the Nittany Lion bench flooded onto the field to engulf Hawkins in jubilation after securing their first Big Ten championship since 2019.

Injury Report

Midfielders Patrick Carragher and Mac Hobbs were sidelined with undisclosed injuries. Jack Iannantuono was shaken up on his third-quarter goal, but returned to the game. Hunter Aquino came off the field in the fourth quarter and didn’t return to the game.

Up Next

With the win, Penn State secures an automatic bid to the NCAA Tournament. Analysts project the Nittany Lions to host a first-round game next weekend. The full bracket will be released at 9:30 p.m. Sunday.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State men’s basketball commit Jamison White signs with Saint Louis
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
After decommitting from Penn State last month, Jamison White found a new team to join.

White has signed with Saint Louis, the team announced Saturday.

Signed ✍️Welcome Home, @jamowhite10!#RollBills pic.twitter.com/gF8olQytnx— Saint Louis Men's Basketball (@SaintLouisMBB) May 2, 2026

White was Penn State’s lone high school signee in the 2026 class back on National Signing Day in November. He committed to the Nittany Lions last September but reopened his recruitment on April 18.

White is a 247Sports composite 3-star in the 2026 class, ranking as the No. 241 player nationally, No. 41 power forward and the No. 2 player from Illinois.

The 6-foot-7, 215-pounder played his senior year at East St. Louis High School. He led his team to an Illinois Class 3A State Championship this season, averaging 19.7 points, 12.8 rebounds and 2.9 blocks per game, per MaxPreps.

White was named the Class 3A All-State team by the Illinois Basketball Coaches Association.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball suffers loss to No. 3 Nebraska in series opener
author: Luci Palacios | For the Collegian
description:
After a road series sweep last weekend, Penn State returned to Happy Valley to take on No. 3 Nebraska, looking to carry its momentum into a tough matchup at home.

Penn State fell to Nebraska 5-1 to start the series.

Sophomore right-hander Abigail Britton started in the circle for the Nittany Lions. Nebraska’s Jordy Frahm started strong with a hit to right center and made it to second, but the next two batters got out.

Ava Kuszak took the plate and hit a line drive to center field, making it to first base. On a wild pitch, Frahm scored, giving the Cornhuskers a 1-0 lead.

Frahm took the circle for the Cornhuskers, and Breanna Hanik hit it to center for a lead-off single. Kaylie Walters hit a sacrifice bunt, and Hanik made it to second.

Already on a roll this season, Allison Oneacre continued it with a grounder to center field, which sent Hanik home to tie the game 1-1. It marked Oneacre’s 59th RBI of the year.

Frahm proceeded to halt the Nittany Lions' offense, striking out two to end the first.

After two outs to start the second, Kacie Hoffmann walked to give the Cornhuskers some momentum. Lauren Camenzind brought up the intensity with an RBI triple to right-center, giving Nebraska a 2-1 lead.

Coach Clarisa Crowell switched pitchers after going through the Nebraska batting order once. Junior McKenna Young got the nod to finish the second inning.

It was quiet for the blue and white when they took the plate and could not get anything going. Neither team could get anything going in the third, leaving the inning scoreless

This continued into the fourth as both teams put their dominant defenses on display.

Nebraska had other plans as Camenzind hit it to center field for a single. This snapped the streak of retiring eight consecutive Cornhuskers for the Nittany Lions.

Frahm stepped to the plate and laid down a sacrifice bunt, allowing Camenzind to reach second. Hannah Coor brought up the excitement with an RBI single, bringing the score to 3-1. That was all for the Cornhuskers in the fifth.

The fifth was quiet for the Nittany Lions as no runs were scored in the frame.

The Cornhuskers kept the momentum from the fifth as Jesse Farrell hit a solo home run, giving Nebraska a three-run lead, though that was all the Cornhuskers could get going in the sixth.

Nebraska put its dominant defense on display in the sixth. Frahm caused Walters to strike out, giving them the first out of the inning.

Oneacre grounded out to second, both her and Hanik failing to reach base, racking up the three outs. Nebraska did not plan to give out any more runs to the Nittany Lions. Another scoreless inning for the blue and white.

To start the seventh, Hoffmann led off with a double, displaying the dominance from both sides of the ball for Nebraska.

Coach Crowell switched pitchers for the last time, putting in McKenzie Duncan.

Pinch Runner Kenndai Williams swapped for Hoffman at second. Coor proceeded to hit it to the outfield and led Williams home for a 5-1 lead. The rest of the inning was quiet for the Cornhuskers.

The Nittany Lions went into the seventh hoping to turn the game around, but Klosowicz hit it to center field and got out. Barbanente followed up with the same fate.

Pinch-hitter Jordan George came in to keep the game alive for the blue and white, but it wasn't enough as the Nittany Lions fell to Nebraska in the first game of the series.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s volleyball knocked out of NCAA Tournament in five-set loss to UC Irvine
author: Chase Mitchell | The Daily Collegian
description:
Mark Pavlik’s final season leading Penn State has been a good one, as he brought his squad to the NCAA Tournament following a fourth consecutive EIVA Championship – the program’s 38th conference title – after finishing the season 22-7 and going undefeated in the conference through 12 matches.

However, there was still another milestone Pavlik and the blue and white were chasing: their first National Championship since 2008.

It just wasn’t written in the stars for the blue and white though, as Pavlik’s illustrious Penn State career was capped off with the Nittany Lions taking it to the wire but falling 3-2 to UC Irvine in the opening round of the NCAA Tournament.

The Anteaters got on the board first in the opening set with a William D’Arcy kill, but a following service error gave the blue and white its first point of the night.

Points were traded back and forth in the following minutes, but a 4-0 UC Irvine scoring run gave the Anteaters an early 10-5 lead, forcing Pavlik and the Nittany Lions into a timeout.

Former Anteater setter and current head coach David Kniffin’s squad tallied five kills en route to its early first set lead.

Five Penn State kills brought the blue and white closer, narrowing the gap to make it a 16-13 Anteater lead as Gaige Gabriel, Owen Rose and Matthew Luoma got their names in on the action.

Down six points with little momentum, Pavlik opted for Penn State’s second timeout of the opening set as outside hitter Sean Harvey looked out of his comfort zone with zero kills on five attacks for a -.600 hitting percentage.

Nothing changed down the stretch as the Anteaters overpowered the blue and white and cruised to a 25-18 opening set victory.

Again, the West Coast squad opened up the scoring in the second set with a Cameron Kosty service ace before Harvey finally got out of his slump, recording his first two kills of the night as a part of a 6-1 blue and white scoring run that gave it the early 6-3 lead before the Anteaters called its first timeout.

Another Harvey kill – his fourth of the night – extended Penn State’s lead to five points before UC Irvine turned on the jets and came storming back, cutting its deficit to 12-10 after another Kosty service ace which pushed the Nittany Lions into their first timeout of the second frame.

A 7-3 Anteater scoring run, backed by Andrej Jokanovic’s two service aces and a pair of Andreas Brinck kills, gave them their first lead since the beginning of the set as they jumped out to a 19-16 lead with the end of the second set in their sights.

Three consecutive blue and white kills made things interesting again as the scoring tied the set at 21-21 with outside hitter Bennett Wilson notching a pair of kills and Tristan Hassell getting in the mix as well.

The action came to a standstill when the score reached 25-25, but after some battling back-and-forth through some kills, blocks and errors, Penn State captured the second frame, winning 29-27 with Rose sealing it on his second service ace of the night.

Outside of his aces, Rose leads the nation in total blocks, entering Friday’s match with 152, leading to Penn State being the No. 1 blocking team in the nation.

It was an indecisive start to the third set, with UC Irvine taking an early lead before trading blows with the blue and white as six consecutive kills led to a 6-6 draw as Harvey moved up to 10 kills on the night while D’arcy improved his total to seven.

A third lead change gave the Nittany Lions the 11-10 lead as the Anteaters called for a timeout after back-to-back Wilson service aces marked the third and fourth of the set overall.

The Anteaters won a challenge for a block touch call that originally made it a 17-14 Penn State lead, but the reversal changed the score to 16-15 with UC Irvine facing just a one point deficit.

What started as utter domination for UC Irvine slowly turned into a match that looked like the Nittany Lions were in control as they extended their lead to 22-17 in the third frame after another Wilson service ace and more kills for Harvey and Hassell.

Hassell’s seventh kill of the night put a bow on the frame and Penn State, after winning the third set 25-19, was suddenly in prime position to pick up an opening round victory with one set to go.

With authority, the Anteaters got out to a commanding 6-2 lead in the fourth set in what felt like just a few seconds due to five Nittany Lion errors on their serves and attack efforts.

After an early Penn State timeout, UC Irvine extended its lead to 11-6 as D’arcy picked up his 11th kill of the night, hitting .455% along the way.

Just like the first set, the fourth was all UC Irvine from start to finish as it captured the frame 25-17, highlighted by Micah Goss picking up his 21st service ace of the season and Jokanovic notching his 11th kill of the night.

The Nittany Lions got out in front in the payoff fifth set, jumping out to a 3-1 lead after Hassell recorded a kill and a service ace on back-to-back plays.

After Kosty took a hit to the face, blood coming from his nose caused a lengthy delay as he was being attended to for a possible broken nose, with officials ordering him to wash his hands before being allowed back in the game.

With the lengthy medical delay in the books, three consecutive Anteater scores tied the action at 6-6, perhaps with the Nittany Lions being jarred from the pause in the action.

In the shortened fifth set, UC Irvine was the first squad to reach 10 points after a Harvey attack error with Goss and Kosty on the block assist.

A Trevor Clark kill gave the Anteaters a 13-12 lead as the action was coming to a head, with UC Irvine eying a hard-fought victory.

In the end, the Anteaters did just enough to hang on as a Goss kill sealed the deal to capture the fifth set 15-13, marking a 3-2 victory to advance to the regional final against UCLA and send Penn State packing.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Zara Larsson delivers nostalgia-fueled night at Movin' On Festival
author: Brennan Kramp I The Daily Collegian
description:
The headlining act for Penn State’s Movin’ On Festival, Zara Larsson, took the stage during a dark and drizzly Friday night to a crowd that went absolutely ballistic as she strutted onto the stage.

Zara Larsson, a Swedish pop star, has had her Penn State fans in a frenzy since the moment they knew she would be headlining the festival. As attendees walked down University Drive to the event, there was an unrelenting chatter and excitement around being able to see Larsson live.

“I’ve been listening to Zara Larsson since I was 10 years old, my younger self would be so freaking happy that I’m here right now,” Anjali Chungapally, a third-year studying civil engineering, said.

Since Larsson has been performing for over a decade, she has a massive nostalgia appeal. Since she reminds her fans of listening to the pop station in the car with their parents and scrolling on TikTok at the height of the COVID era, they were overjoyed at seeing Larsson in the flesh.

Her music is pure pop at its finest. Her mix of slow jams and heavy hitting bangers have put her at the top of people’s playlists for years. It’s just part of what keeps her fans coming back to her music.

When Larsson took center stage, every set of hands in the audience raised simultaneously, phones firmly grasped, ready to record every word of her opening song. The crowd roared constantly, for several songs, as Larsson hypnotized the crowd with her confidence and poise.

After falling out of the public eye for a brief stint, Penn State students were pleased that Movin’ On chose to make her the headliner for this year's festival. Nostalgia, being the key factor in her fandom, helped garner a packed festival grounds with fans packed tightly and jumping to the beat.

“We haven’t really had a musician in our generation that's fallen off and then made a comeback like her,” Zanah Absi, a fourth-year studying hospitality management, said. “She has a lot of nostalgic fans.”

Larsson’s discography is extensive, featuring many chart topping songs and becoming recognizable through a multitude of platforms. With so many songs to choose from, every fan had a different tune they were excited for the most.

“There’s so many I want to hear, it’s really hard for me to pick just one,” Lona Horner, a third-year studying veterinary and biomedical sciences, said.

Larsson’s final song, “Symphony,” a popular hit on social media and radiowaves, had the crowd in a trance. Phone flashlights and cameras created a lightshow in front of the stage, and every attendee in the crowd sang along with an undying fervor until the very last note.

Then, they erupted in cheers, applause and even tears.

“Me and my girlfriend listened to her all the time when she got big again, so it was really fun seeing her, especially here at Penn State…just awesome,” Sam Otis, a third-year studying corporate innovation and entrepreneurship, said. “It seemed like a lot of fans here were way more excited than us though, I think this concert meant a lot to a lot of people.”

MORE MOVIN' ON COVERAGE

title: That Mexican OT brings Texas to Movin’ On
author: Justin Elliott | For the Collegian
description:
That Mexican OT brought the heart of Texan-Mexican, or “Tejano,” hip-hop to the stage Friday evening for the 51st annual Movin’ On festival.

The Bay City, Texas native broke into the hip-hop scene in 2023, setting himself apart with a distinct mix of rural southern rap and Tejano culture.

The Mexican-American rapper took the stage dressed in a white T-shirt tucked into cowboy-cut Wrangler jeans and a belt, along with glasses and a “Texas-style” mullet. He also threw on a cowboy hat from Christopher Ramos, a first-year mechanical engineering student near the front of the crowd. Ramos was also dressed in a cowboy-inspired outfit.

“I came here for the OT, bro,” he said.

That Mexican OT opened his set with an a capella performance of a verse from his song “Cowboy Killer,” before performing the full song with his DJ. The rapper used this a cappella opening method frequently, getting the crowd familiar with the hard-hitting rap songs before properly performing them.

Despite the chilly, wet weather, the rapper kept everyone moving, performing fan-favorite songs like “Hit List,” “V-Man,” “02.02.99” and his hit single, “Johnny Dang.”

Several attendees, including Carter Colley who came from Bradford, PA, said Johnny Dang was their favorite part of the performance.

“I was pretty hyped for it,” Colley said. “I think the crowd could be louder.”

Roo Harris, a first-year studying biology at York College of Pennsylvania, traveled to see the rapper live for the first time. The rapper took Harris’ phone to record selfie-style videos of his performance with the audience, DJ and fellow on-stage artists.

“It was good, it was really good,” said Harris, who sang and danced along with the rapper throughout the set.

The most distinct part of the rapper’s set, which featured 16 songs, was his interactions with the crowd between each song. From wearing fans’ hats, to telling stories related to the songs, to bringing a shirtless fan on stage to dance, he kept the crowd engaged.

21-year-old landscaper Jayden Gearhart said he liked this year’s Movin’ On festival better than last year’s because of That Mexican OT.

“I love how he was interacting,” Gearhart said. “So far, he’s definitely the most [interactive].”

The rapper’s transitions between his infectious, hard-hitting songs were deliberate and elicited laughter from the crowd at times, such as when he pointed out a person at the top of the festival’s ferris wheel, before performing his song, “Point Em Out.”

The audience also taught him Penn State’s “We Are” chant, which he did several times.

Jessica Gallardo Caballero, a second-year forensic science major, came to the festival with Ramos. The two attendees started listening to the rapper at the same time after learning about him through the festival announcement.

“I love hearing different music styles from people that are from my culture,” said Gallardo Caballero, who brought a Mexican flag from her wall at home, waving it throughout the set. The rapper eventually brought the flag on stage, signing it and draping it along the DJ table behind him.

“When I saw him sign it, I started crying,” she said. “I had this in my room — I love my culture so much.”

That Mexican OT concluded his set with a short story about his mother who had him at 18 years old and told people he would become a successful rapper.

“Thank y’all for making my mother’s dream come true,” he said before leaving the stage.

FOR MORE MOVIN' ON CONTENT

title: ‘She has a really great way of captivating the entire crowd’ | Slayyyter performs at 2026 Movin’ On Festival
author: Paloma Munoz | The Daily Collegian
description:
Hyperpop singer Slayyyter performed at the IM fields on Friday as part of Penn State’s Movin’ On. The energy in the field was ecstatic regardless of the pouring rain as she walked out for her first song, “DANCE…”

Attendees were excited for her performance, especially since many of them watched her rise to popularity on social media.

“I’m on Twitter a lot, and they’ve been pushing ‘CRANK’ since last summer,” Luke Snyder, a fourth-year studying meteorology and broadcast journalism, said. “So, when I saw that Slayyyter was one of the acts here, I was absolutely thrilled. She has a really great way of captivating the entire crowd, no matter where you are, and captivating everyone’s energy.”

The rain and cold didn’t stop that energy, either. Concert-goers danced as the set continued on for about 45 minutes, with Slayyyter performing many of her popular hits from her most recent album “Wor$t Girl in America,” including “CRANK” and “OLD TECHNOLOGY.” She finished her set with her most popular song, “BEAT UP CHANEL$,” and also performed her month-old single, “BROKE — FREE$TYLE.”

Many concert-goers were wearing bunny ears, a signature image for the singer's “Wor$t Girl in America” album.

“The energy was really crazy,” Adithi Gollakota, a fourth-year student studying computer science, said. “We came in while ‘CRANK’ was happening, so we got all the energy from the beginning.”

Gollakota said she hasn’t listened to Slayyyter much but was excited by the set.

“I’ve heard her album before, but I’m definitely going to go listen to her more after this” she said.

Slayyyter is a pop singer, specifically within a niche genre known as “hyperpop,”a high-energy version of pop music. She mixes the genre with varied vocals and even incorporates heavy metal influence in some of her songs. Many fans admire her voice and its range.

“‘Worst Girl in America’ came out, and it was just, like, on repeat,” Alexandra Roushinko, a first-year studying film production, said. “I knew about her a little bit in 2019 because I dabbled in hyperpop and then after her resurgence, I was like, ‘I know this girl,’ and so I listened to it and I was like ‘oh she’s the best singer alive.’”

As the set finished off, many concert goers screamed compliments at Slayyyter, with many more talking and still dancing, even though the music cut off. As the crowd started to disperse, many took pictures with the stage, which still had some of the visuals from the show.

“I absolutely adore Slayyyter,” Snyder said. “She is a phenomenal performer. She has been growing her presence since Coachella – phenomenal act, tons of energy, great way to open Movin’ On this year.”

FOR MORE MOVIN' ON COVERAGE

title: Penn State track and field's Allon Clay breaks 1,500m school record
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Allon Clay is a two-time school record holder.

Clay broke the school record in the men's 1,500-meter in the Mike Brady Elite Men's 1,500m at the Larry Ellis Invitational on Friday, running 3:37.84.

The previous record of 3:38.32 was set by Brannon Kidder in 2014, who was a world champion in 2017.

Clay now has the school record in both the 1,500m and 1,000-meters after running 2:17.29 at the David Hemery Valentine Invite in February.

MORE TRACK AND FIELD COVERAGE

title: Rain or shine, Amelia & Sequoia rock the stage at Movin’ On
author: Brennan Kramp I The Daily Collegian
description:
Student band Amelia & Sequoia were the featured openers for Penn State’s student-run music festival, Movin’ On. Every year, Movin’ On grants one student-based band to open the festival, and this year Amelia & Sequoia had the opportunity to get the crowd going with some local tunes.

Through the dreary rain, Amelia & Sequoia were able to put on a stellar performance and set a high bar for the remaining acts.

“I think the rain made it even better; you had to be a true fan to be out here,” Joe Korsak, a third-year studying computer science, said.

The rain started at the beginning of the band’s set, and co-lead singers Aiden Hood and Amelia Severns appeared unfazed by the cold, despite sporting cut-off Led Zeppelin t-shirts.

The set continued with all smiles from the band and a groovy pace. The crowd was a sea of ponchos and jackets, as the tiny crowd swayed and bobbed along to the rhythm of the band. The movement never stopped.

“It was really cold, so it was hard for me to dance, but I kept moving anyway,” Lily Chancey, a second-year studying theatre, said.

Amelia & Sequoia is actually the combination of two different musical entities. As a unit, they travel as the band, Sequoia, alongside solo artist, Amelia Severns. Their music is a combination of alternative, jazz and soul with tones of 70s rock.

Each member of the band had an opportunity to show off their skill sets in different ways. From the saxophone, to the bass, to the keyboard, the band gave their all to put forth a unique sound that made for an even more exciting experience.

Saxophonist Braeden Caputo enthralled the crowd with a superb solo before handing off the spotlight to Rayne Terrier, who rattled off his best Flea impression on a funky and captivating bassline. Keyboardist, James Paul, got his fair share of limelight, too, as his disco-inspired flow made the crowd scream in awe.

Penn Staters weren’t the only people in attendance either.

Brian Beere, a third year studying integrated media arts at Juniata College, spoke about the lovely sounds of Amelia & Sequoia. This was his first time attending Movin’ On, and he was greeted with some local tunes.

“The synth was incredible, I really liked the talkbox they used, that was really cool,” Beere said. “I haven't seen many bands do that. The saxophone solo was also incredible, and the two lead vocalists, their voices just worked together so well.”

For the band, being the opening act for the Movin’ On festival was just one of the many proud steps they have taken as a group in recent weeks.

The night prior to their opening act, Severns released her newest single on Spotify, “Blame Myself.” Before that release, the band collaborated with Severns on a few other songs that they plan to unveil soon.

Now, being the opener for a prided Penn State tradition, they stamp themselves in the history books as part of something they will look back on for years to come.

“We just want to thank you guys so much for staying out here in the rain, it means so much that you guys decided to stick around for us,” Hood said.

MORE MOVIN' ON CONTENT

title: May Day rally calls on Penn State to end contracts with Enterprise, Flock Safety over ICE connections
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
Several Penn State unions, in conjunction with local groups and community members, held a rally by the Allen Street Gates on Friday to observe May Day.

May Day, originally known as International Workers’ Day, commemorates the actions of workers during the May 4, 1886 Haymarket affair and the demands for an eight-hour workday. The day is not officially recognized in the United States but is still observed nationwide to highlight the continued struggle for equal working rights.

Members of organizations such as Penn State’s chapter of the American Association of University Professors (AAUP), Food Not Bombs, Central PA United and SEIU Local 668 – also known as the Penn State Faculty Alliance (PSFA) – chanted slogans like “This is what democracy looks like” and “Dump Trump,” held signs and carried flags.

Organizers also promoted a petition directed at Penn State President Neeli Bendapudi, calling on the university to end its contracts with Enterprise Rent-A-Car and Flock Safety by June 1, 2026. The petition is a response to the use of Enterprise’s vehicles and Flock’s cameras in Immigrations & Customs Enforcement (ICE) operations nationwide.

Karl Nordstrom, who is organizing a student offshoot of Keystone Indivisible called Nittany Indivisible, said he is concerned about the potential for ICE to take advantage of Penn State’s connections to Enterprise and Flock.

“ICE and related groups use Enterprise and Flock cameras are looking at license plates and maybe facial identification,” Nordstrom said. “Sometimes, the people that have the Flock cameras let ICE have access to them and they're using it for tracking people that they want to detain.”

While Nordstrom may not know how Penn State will receive the petition, he said he still thinks it's important for him and others to voice their objections to the university’s dealings.

“I don't have control over the university,” Nordstrom said. “All I know is that I can make an effort and if they respond, well, great. It's worth a try.”

Jo Dumas, a member of Penn State AAUP, said she was especially concerned about the potential for Flock cameras and other surveillance technologies to infringe on the rights of Americans. She hopes this is conveyed by the petition.

“When it's submitted to the president of our university, hopefully, she will understand that everybody on that petition has a problem with that contract and the work that it's doing in the process of surveilling our people,” Dumas, an associate teaching professor in the Donald P. Bellisario College of Communications, said.

Dumas said she hoped the rally inspires hope in the power of organized labor to negotiate with their employers.

“That's why we've got limitations on hours,” Dumas said. “That is why we've got healthcare benefits and pension benefits, because of organized labor requiring that of employers through contracts that organized laborers negotiate.”

Andy McKinnon, a member of the Nittany Valley Environmental Coalition, said he saw May Day as an effective way to make the issue of workers’ rights harder to ignore.

“May Day is something that I think is speaking to the need to get out on the streets on a regular basis, not just today,” McKinnon said. “We need to get the ball rolling, the momentum gathered to actually get unions established to actually get the focus put back on workers, as opposed to having corporations and companies just running the show and exploiting workers at every turn.”

He also said that despite seeing the petition as a good way to protest Penn State’s connections to companies that support ICE, he believes the only way to incite change on this issue is an organized effort to directly affect the university.

“The halls of power are not gonna change on their own, no matter how much we plead with them,” McKinnon said. “We have to hurt Penn State economically in terms of, probably, that societal awareness that's burgeoning, that's increasing over a period of time, hopefully to a critical mass and reaches that point where Penn State actually starts to lose students.”

MORE NEWS COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball postpones Friday night game against Minnesota
author: Ashley Felsberg I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State's series opener against Minnesota has been postponed.

Due to inclement weather in Happy Valley, the Nittany Lions will start the series with the Golden Gophers with a double-header starting at noon on Saturday.

🚨Schedule Adjustment🚨Tonight's game against Minnesota has been rained out.We will begin the series with a doubleheader tomorrow beginning at noon.#WeAre pic.twitter.com/fK3TtdGijQ— Penn State Baseball (@PennStateBASE) May 1, 2026

Game Two will begin approximately 35 minutes after the conclusion of Game One. Game Three will be played Sunday at 11:30 am, as originally scheduled.

The weekend's promotional schedule has also been adjusted. After each game was set to price the first 2,000 hot dogs at $1, Saturday's doubleheader will see an unlimited number of "Dollar Dogs" available for purchase.

Sad news: tonight's game has been rained outHappy news: Unlimited Dollar Dogs tomorrow!!! Doubleheader at noon!#WeAre pic.twitter.com/COauTtEP8m— Penn State Baseball (@PennStateBASE) May 1, 2026

The blue and white last saw the Gophers this past season, and escaped with a 3-game sweep.

Penn State is now 12-29 on the season, and is looking for late-season wins to qualify for the Big Ten Tournament. Minnesota is 27-17 overall.

author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Due to inclement weather, Penn State won't be taking Beard Field on Friday night.

The Nittany Lions series opener against No. 3 Nebraska is now slated for 11 a.m. Saturday.

Due to persistent inclement weather in Central Pennsylvania, tonight's game against #3 Nebraska has been canceled. PSU will now face the Cornhuskers in a doubleheader on Saturday at 11 a.m. (ET) and 2:30 p.m. (ET) from Beard Field.#WeAre | #AllIn— Penn State Softball (@PennStateSB) May 1, 2026

title: Former Penn State women's basketball assistant coach Daniel Barber promoted to associate head coach at Arizona State
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
A former Penn State coach is earning a promotion.

Former Lady Lion assistant coach Daniel Barber was promoted to associate head coach at Arizona State on Friday.

Congratulations to @CoachBarber24 for being promoted to Associate Head Coach! pic.twitter.com/84jB2iFJKF— Sun Devil WBB (@SunDevilWBB) May 1, 2026

Barber spent the last season with the Sun Devils as an assistant coach, where he helped first-year head coach Molly Miller produce the team's first NCAA Tournament appearance since 2019.

title: USA Hockey invites Katie DeSa, Morgan Stickney to Goaltending and Women’s Defense Development Camp
author: Kippi Hall | The Daily Collegian
description:
USA Hockey is getting a taste of Nittany Lions between the posts.

Past 🤝 Future Katie DeSa and incoming Nittany Lion Morgan Stickney are headed to USA Hockey’s National Goaltending and Women’s Defense Development Camp later this month 🥅🇺🇸#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/usN52BbQha— Penn State Women’s Hockey (@PennStateWHKY) May 1, 2026

USA Hockey has formally invited Katie DeSa and incoming Nittany Lion Morgan Strickney to the National Goaltending and Women’s Defense Development Camp. The camp is set to take place from May 18-21 in Blaine, Minnesota.

DeSa, a senior from Pawcatuck, Connecticut, finished the 2025-26 season with a 27-6-0 record and 629 saves. Her .936 save percentage as a senior ranked tenth nationally, and 27 victories set a school record for most single-season wins in program history.

Stickney, an incoming freshman from Manhattan Beach, California, is coming from the USA U18 Women’s Team with a 2-0-0 record in the Women’s World Championship this year.

title: Matt Campbell set to throw first pitch at Penn State softball game on Saturday
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Matt Campbell will make an appearance in front of the Penn State faithful this weekend, but it will come on the mound instead of the gridiron.

Campbell is set to throw the ceremonial first pitch at Beard Field for the game against No. 2 Nebraska at 2:30 p.m. Saturday

Ahead of his first season as Penn State’s head coach, Campbell has previously dropped the ceremonial faceoff at the men’s hockey team’s first-ever game at Beaver Stadium on Jan. 31 against Michigan State.

The day prior, he made a public appearance at the Bryce Jordan Center when the wrestling team took on the Cornhuskers in a dual meet, receiving a standing ovation when he was introduced to the crowd of 13,225 spectators.

Campbell’s been making it a point to get out into the community as well, recently attending the Happy Valley United golf outing on Monday at West Shore Country Club, where he spoke to the fans and alumni.

He joins the trend of honored Nittany Lions throwing ceremonial pitches at softball and baseball games, including the new men’s soccer coach Rob Dow and Olympic gold-medalist Tessa Janecke of the women’s hockey team.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: No. 15 Penn State men’s lacrosse set to rematch No. 7 Johns Hopkins for Big Ten Tournament title
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 15 Penn State is on to the Big Ten Championship game after sweeping the season-long series over No. 11 Maryland, last year’s national runner-ups, with a gritty 8-6 victory in the Big Ten semifinals.

The second-seeded Nittany Lions are set to square off against first-seeded, and seventh-ranked team in the nation, Johns Hopkins on Saturday at noon in Piscataway, New Jersey.

Tale of the tape

Penn State’s win over the Terrapins on Thursday evening sent the Nittany Lions back to the Big Ten Championship after falling short to Maryland in last year’s semifinal. Last time Penn State played for the title, Michigan handled business with a 16-4 victory in Columbus, Ohio.

Overall, the blue and white enter the conference final with an 8-5 record, and are 4-2 in the Big Ten — including Thursday’s win over Maryland. Penn State also has wins over other highly-ranked programs, such as No. 2 Princeton and No. 4 Cornell.

Meanwhile, Johns Hopkins defeated the Wolverines, 14-12, only a few hours earlier at SHI Stadium. The Blue Jays pace the Big Ten with a 9-4 overall record and a 4-2 mark in the conference, edging out the Nittany Lions in the standings due to head-to-head formalities.

Earlier in the regular season, Johns Hopkins got the better of Penn State on April 4, where the Nittany Lions fell 11-10 in a nail-biting affair. The blue and white led by five goals in the third quarter, but let the lead slip away in the dying moments.

With both teams in the Big Ten Championship game, their positions in the national tournament should be secured. However, if Penn State fails to take home the title, there is the slight chance that they could barely miss the cut. Johns Hopkins’ higher ranking, however, should be enough to carry the Blue Jays into the tournament regardless of the title game’s result.

Players to watch

In Penn State’s two recent games, it’s been all about Hunter Aquino, who returned from an injury late in the regular season and is now rediscovering his groove on offense. He’s been the primary goal-scorer since returning to form, putting up five points in the regular-season finale against Rutgers and a hat trick in the Big Ten semifinal. He’s joined by the newly-named Big Ten Freshman of the Year, Jack Iannantuono, who was noticeably quiet with only a single assist against the Terrapins, but still leads the team with 33 points.

To round out the attacking core, Kyle Lehman and Liam Matthews cycle through for a four-man rotation. Lehman and Aquino share a team-high 21 goals on the campaign, despite Aquino missing out on roughly half the season due to injuries.

At midfielder, Chase Robertson takes the majority of the shots, letting seven fly against Maryland despite none going on the scoreboard. Michael Faraone leads the midfield with 17 goals on the season, immediately followed by Robertson’s 16.

Alex Ross highlights the defense in his final collegiate season before joining the New York Atlas of the Premier Lacrosse League, with a team-high 20 caused turnovers and 29 ground ball pickups. He stands guard over goaltender Preston Hawkins, a true freshman, who made eight saves on 14 shots in his first collegiate tournament game.

On the other hand, attackman Hunter Chauvette led the Blue Jays to victory over Michigan with three goals and a pair of assists in the semifinals, including the game-winning goal with 7:51 remaining in the fourth quarter. He ranks first on the team with 33 goals and second with 38 points, one shy of midfielder Matt Collison’s 39.

Johns Hopkins’ defense is led by a short-stick defensive midfielder, Reece DiCicco, who leads the team with 51 ground balls and 21 caused turnovers. Luke Martin and Carson Brown are the top down-low defenders, combining to make the Blue Jays’ defense difficult to penetrate.

Oran Gelinas mans the goal for Johns Hopkins and posts a .571 save percentage and 9.32 goals-against average as one of the nation’s top goaltenders.

title: Former Penn State women's basketball coach Adrienne Motley hired at Ole Miss
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Former Penn State coach Adrienne Motley is heading down south.

Motley was hired as an assistant coach at Ole Miss on Friday, reuniting with former Lady Lion Rachael Okokoh.

Welcome to the Sip, Coach Motley! 📈#HottyToddy x @Coach_Mot23 pic.twitter.com/7OOY1r6VPq— Ole Miss Women's BB (@OleMissWBB) May 1, 2026

Motley spent three years with Penn State, including two as an assistant coach. There, she primarily worked with the guards, and served as director of player development in her first year.

Prior to her time at Penn State, Motley played on various professional teams in Europe, where she was named 2023 Romanian League Guard of the Year.

Motley graduated from Miami, where she played under former Penn State coach Carolyn Kieger, who served as an assistant coach. The Newport News, Virginia, native was a four-time All-ACC honoree before being drafted 32nd in the WNBA draft.

Motley will join the Rebels as an assistant coach following a season where they earned a second round appearance in the NCAA Tournament.

title: Penn State earns commitment from WR Jamir Dean
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Matt Campbell has picked up commitments in back-to-back days.

3-star wide receiver Jamir Dean has committed to Penn State, he announced via X on Friday.

Dean commits just one day after defensive lineman Stanley Montgomery pledged to the Nittany Lions, as the recruiting trail remains hot for Campbell and company.

Dean ranks as the No. 433 player in the country, the No. 54 wide receiver and the No. 18 player in Tennessee.

The 2027 commit recently visited Happy Valley on April 23, committing just nine days later. He has an official visit scheduled for the weekend of June 19.

Penn State beat out Arkansas, Purdue and Wisconsin for the 3-star Tennessee product in the 2027 class.
