title: Election Guide: What to know ahead of the 2026 Pennsylvania primary election
author: Jacob Godshall I The Daily Collegian
description:
On May 19, Pennsylvania’s primary elections will take place. This election gives voters a chance to decide which candidates will represent their political party in the general election in November.

May 4 is the deadline to register to vote in Pennsylvania primaries. May 12 is the last day to apply for an absentee or mail-in ballot. On May 19, polls will be open from 7 a.m. to 8 p.m.

Voting in the PA Primaries

Party Affiliation

Pennsylvania is one of eight states with a fully-closed primary election, meaning voters will only be able to vote in races within their own party.

Independent or unaffiliated voters will be unable to vote in either party’s primaries, but will still be able to answer ballot questions.

Polling Locations

Polling locations will be available throughout the state. Find your polling location here. For students living on campus, the polling location is in the HUB-Robeson Center.

What if I can’t vote in-person?

Any registered voter can apply for an absentee ballot if they are unable to make it to their polling location due to illness, disability, work or travel.

Alternatively, any registered voter may apply for a mail-in ballot. Mail-in ballots must be received by 8 p.m on May 19 and can be mailed back or dropped off at the polling location in person.

For more information on mail-in and absentee ballots, visit here.

Who can vote in the primaries?

Any Pennsylvania resident over the age of 18 who is registered to vote may participate in the 2026 primaries, with a few small exceptions.

Party Affiliations

The most restrictive exception, voters may only participate in their registered party’s primaries. Unaffiliated and third-party voters will still have the opportunity to vote on any ballot questions.

Age/Location Restrictions: To vote in any primary election, the voter must be 18 and resident of the state they plan to vote in (and the United States) for at least 30 days.

Felons: Most Pennsylvania voters with a felony will be able to vote in the primaries, but any who are currently in incarceration for a felony conviction will be unable to.

What do I need to bring?

If this is your first time voting at your polling location, you will need a valid ID from the list below.

This includes: driver’s licenses, U.S. passports, government issued IDs, student IDs, paychecks, bank statements, current utility bills, government issued checks or documents with full names and addresses, voter registration cards or firearm permits.

Who is up for election?

Governor

Current Pennsylvania Gov. Josh Shapiro is running unopposed in the Democratic primaries. He will be challenged by Republican Stacy Garrity, who is also running unopposed in the Republican primaries.

Shapiro has served as Pennsylvania’s governor since he was sworn in in 2023. He’s campaigning on public education investments, job creation and the representation of all Pennsylvanians, according to his campaign website.

Republican candidate Stacy Garrity, current state treasurer, has expressed support for natural gas drilling. Her campaign website lists her as a “strong ally” of President Donald Trump. She is campaigning on transparency, fraud elimination and cutting government waste.

15th congressional district

Both Democrat Ray Bilger and Republican Glenn Thompson are running unopposed in the primary race, and will face each other in November.

Bigler has expressed his personal connection to the area throughout his campaign, having grown up in Bellefonte. He is campaigning on affordability, increased health care and infrastructure. Additionally, he wants to bring “hope and opportunity” to the district, according to his campaign website.

Thompson has served as the representative from the 15th Congressional District since 2019. Before that, he was the representative from the 5th Congressional District from 2009 to 2018. Per his website, He is campaigning on supporting agriculture and farmers, rural development and increased infrastructure in rural areas.

82nd Legislative District

Both Paul Takac (D-Centre) and Republican Kirsten McTernan are running unopposed in the primary race, and will face each other in November.

Paul Takac has been the 82nd district’s representative since 2022. Takac is committed to clean and renewable energy in Pennsylvania, accessible health care and bringing resources to rural communities, according to his website.

After running for State College school board in 2025, McTernan will be running to represent the 82nd district in November. McTernan has not shared much publicly about her goals or priorities.

Member of Democratic State Committee

Candidates Laura Shadle, Joanne Totsi-Vasey and Jeremy S. Breon are running for re-election. Breon currently serves as the Prothonotary and Clerk of Courts, while Shadle and Totsi-Vasey are general members.

All three candidates will be elected to the committee, as three positions are available for this election.

Member of Republican State Committee

Candidates Rick Smead, Michelle Schellberg and Monica Oakes are running for re-election. Oakes currently serves as the secretary, Schellberg as the chair and Smead as a general member.

All three candidates listed on the ballot will be elected to the committee, as three positions are available for this election.

MORE CAMPUS COVERAGE

title: Predicting 2026 Penn State defense’s post-spring practice depth chart
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State’s defense, just like its offense, has dealt with some injuries from the end of last season and into the spring practices that leaves the projected depth chart still unknown.

However, after 15 spring practices, here’s a prediction of the defensive depth chart heading into next season.

Defensive end

1. Yvan Kemajou, sophomore

2. Max Granville, redshirt sophomore

3. Alex McPherson, sophomore

4. Ike Ezeogu, redshirt senior

Penn State’s defensive end room still wasn’t at full strength at the end of spring practices, as Max Granville has been working back from multiple different injuries. However, the unit showed what the depth chart could look like.

Yvan Kemajou burned his redshirt last year as one of the key contributing freshmen on the team, proving himself as a reliable pass rusher. This year, he’ll enter as one of, if not the top pass rushers on the team, with the defensive end room holding a more youthful trend.

Granville is the other projected starter and very well could be the top pass rusher, but his production is strictly speculatory right now after he redshirted and spent all of last season recovering from a season-ending injury from last May.

Alex McPherson and Ike Ezeogu slot in as the two backups as two guys who’ve received more playing time in their previous schools than either Granville or Kemajou have so far at Penn State. Those two will provide more experience and allow for the pass rushers to rotate in with previous action at the college level.

Defensive tackle

1. Keanu Williams, redshirt senior

2. Armstrong Nnodim, redshirt sophomore

3. Siale Taupaki, redshirt senior +

4. Dallas Vakalahi, junior

Unlike the defensive end room, the defensive tackle group is flooded with older players. The two UCLA transfers, Siale Taupaki and Keanu Williams bring significant experience to the room, but they might not be the “starters.”

Williams brings the most production, starting in 11 of UCLA’s 12 games last year, totaling 39 tackles in 2025. Taupaki, on the other hand, didn’t start in as many games, but played in all 12, only notching two tackles for loss.

Armstrong Nnodim has been the riser in spring practice as a power rusher and disrupter of the group. His size matched with his ability to wreak havoc came alive at Blue-White Practice, making an impact in the 11-on-11 portion of the day. Following the practice, offensive lineman Cooper Cousins mentioned Nnodim as one of the players who talks the most trash on the line, proving his ferocious mentality.

Rounding out the group, Dallas Vakalahi is another defensive tackle who’s bigger, transferring from Utah where he played in nine games and recorded 11 tackles.

No matter who starts in every game, the four defensive tackles will all be contributors at a position that thrives more with added depth.

Linebacker

WILL:

1. Tony Rojas, redshirt junior

2. Cael Brezina, junior

MIKE:

1. Kooper Ebel, senior

2. Alex Tatsch, sophomore

SAM:

1. Caleb Bacon, redshirt senior

2. Rojas

The linebacker position is the group that was the most impacted by Iowa State transfers. However, Tony Rojas will still get the nod as the WILL linebacker when he returns from injury.

With Rojas missing spring practices, the three Iowa State linebackers — Cael Brezina, Caleb Bacon and Kooper Ebel — were all seen on the field together, with Brezina filling in Rojas’ starting spot.

Ebel will be the MIKE linebacker and will likely be the one to get the green dot, as he said after Blue-White Practice he’s been the one primarily wearing it. He’ll also potentially be the top linebacker on the team after tying for the lead in tackles at Iowa State last season.

Alex Tatsch is another player who’s dealt with injuries and will likely be out longer than the rest of the players who’re currently out. While he’s ahead of schedule, it’s expected for him to not be a full-go until much closer to Week 1, and slotting him as the next guy in line as the MIKE after a strong end to the 2025 season, fits him better than the other two linebacker positions.

Cornerback

Outside:

1. Audavion Collins, redshirt senior

2. Daryus Dixson, sophomore

3. Jahmir Joseph, redshirt freshman

Nickel:

1. Zion Tracy, senior

2. Omarion Davis, sophomore

The cornerback room is the one with the most familiarity from last season’s Penn State squad. Audavion Collins, Daryus Dixson and Jahmir Joseph all return as outside cornerbacks and will see a lot of playing time, with Collins and Dixson most likely getting the starting roles.

Collins emerged as one of the starters last year and proved he can hold up on the outside against Big Ten receivers. While Dixson may not be the top guy to start the year, he’ll still start every game and has the potential to be the go-to corner to put against opposing teams’ top targets.

Zion Tracy has received the most hype in the offseason, getting major praise from Campbell throughout spring practices. The nickel spot is his, but he’s someone who can do it all, potentially playing some outside or safety this season.

Joseph will play throughout the year as the next corner behind Collins and Dixson. While he didn’t receive as much playing time as Dixson did in their freshmen campaigns, his five games played were scattered throughout, as he maintained his redshirt before playing significantly more in the Pinstripe Bowl against Clemson.

Omarion Davis, despite being listed as a safety, could play in the nickel position when Tracy isn’t on the field. Davis totaled 13 tackles and a quarterback hurry in his freshman year at Boston College.

Safety

Strong safety:

1. Marcus Neal Jr., junior

2. Vaboue Toure, redshirt sophomore

Free safety:

1. Jeremiah Cooper, redshirt senior

2. Jamison Patton, senior

After the departure of Zakee Wheatley to the NFL draft and King Mack to NC State, the safety position will bring in mostly new faces.

Marcus Neal Jr. still holds the starting strong safety spot. Neal was tied for the lead in tackles with Ebel at Iowa State last season with 77, while also picking off two passes.

On the other side, Jeremiah Cooper will bring speed to the position, having played both cornerback and safety during his time with the Cyclones. In February, he confirmed he’ll be playing safety with the Nittany Lions, the position he had more success at with Iowa State.

Behind them, Vaboue Toure returns to Penn State for his redshirt sophomore season, having been behind multiple players who’re now in the NFL. Jamison Patton, while potentially not starting, will be a player who sees the field frequently in his final year.

MORE FOOTBALL CONTENT

title: From outfits to essentials, students share how they prepare for Movin’ On
author: Serina Chan I For the Collegian
description:
With the semester coming to a close, students are getting ready for Movin’ On, Penn State’s annual, student-run music festival set for Friday at the IM fields from 3:30 p.m. to 10:30 p.m.

Four national acts — Zara Larsson, the Dare, That Mexican OT and Slayyyter — and one local BATTLE winner will perform, meaning students get a range of music genres and styles to see at the event. While excitement is settling over campus, some students are also juggling the balance of academics right before finals week.

Ariana Canariato said she’s planning to study beforehand to ensure her academics come first, and her amusement comes second. In the past week leading up to the festival, she’s been making sure her work gets done.

“I think it’s definitely okay to take a break and go have fun, especially before finals week,” Ariana Canariato, a second-year studying veterinary and biomedical sciences, said.

Canariato also said she’s excited to dress up for the music festival, and that her planned outfit is inspired by headliner Zara Larsson. Larsson is in an era of bright colors and glittery costumes after her 2025 release, “Midnight Sun,” ignited a maximalist, sun-saturated aesthetic across social media — think Lisa Frank and Malibu Barbie.

“[I’m] maybe wearing some type of jean and making the outfit summery,” Canariato said. “I also have claw clips that fit the vibe of the concert.”

Emma Dorko also said the outfit she picked out is also inspired by Larsson. The overall concert atmosphere played a large role in her decision too.

“I think it’s going to be 50/50 in terms of whether people dress up or down,” Dorko, a fourth-year studying animal science, said. “I might wear jeans because it’s going to be colder. But I want to match myself to Zara Larsson’s concert.”

Issy Beck said she and her friends are definitely planning on dressing up in a style similar to that seen at Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival, which is as much about the fashion as it is the music.

“My friends and I would make the whole festival a huge thing,” Beck, a second-year studying biobehavioral health, said. “I would wear mini shorts, boots and a jersey that would be tucked in. I would also want to bring my digital camera.”

Olivia Yogus said she’s planned what emergency items to bring instead of her outfit ahead of time.

“I would bring a portable charger,” Yogus, a second-year studying biology, said. “I don’t know how long I’m going to be there for, so it would be good to have on me.”

Michael Noel, however, said he isn’t planning to bring anything besides his wallet and phone.

“Last year it rained,” Noel, a second-year studying mechanical engineering, said. “I don’t want to have a lot of stuff in my pockets that I could lose or fall out.”

Noel also said he’s dressing for comfort, not for style.

“It depends on the weather,” Noel said. “If it’s raining, I would wear a long sleeve shirt and jeans. I would dress the way I normally do.”

Samuel Endres said the mosh pit that usually opens up at the festival is influencing his outfits plans. He said the heat from all the bodies packed together can make wearing clothes typically meant for colder weather difficult.

“I’ll probably wear brown Carhartt pants, flip flops and a black t-shirt,” Endres, a second-year studying kinesiology, said. “If I get hot or if something gets on my shirt, it won’t be super noticeable if I dress simple.”

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: Film review | Analyzing Penn State women’s basketball’s newest forwards Cristen Carter, Petra Bozan
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
As coach Tanisha Wright continues to build her squad at Penn State after starting with zero players due to the transfer portal and graduation, she added two valuable positions to end last week.

Wright added her first two forwards on her squad in Georgetown’s Cristen Carter and Nebraska’s Petra Božan on Friday and Saturday, completing a full lineup as Bozan became the fifth player on the team.

Both Carter and Bozan have several years of collegiate experience with the Cornhuskers, Hoyas and Bulldogs, creating a veteran presence amongst the Lady Lion forwards.

Cristen Carter

After starting 30 games between two seasons at Butler, 6-foot-4 Cristen Carter took her talents to Georgetown, averaging nearly just as many minutes in only five starts.

While Carter only averaged 4.6 points per game during her junior year, her scoring came with great playmaking.

Against one of the best teams in the nation in UConn, Carter cut to the basket wide open, receiving an assist to make the easy layup.

Georgetown transfer Cristen Carter evades the UConn defense, receiving an assist for a wide open layup under the basket pic.twitter.com/vtyCiMZDTI— Noah Aberegg (@aberegg_noah) April 30, 2026

Despite recording less than one assist per game in each collegiate season, Carter is formidable when passing the ball out to her teammates.

In a similar play, Carter found herself on the passing end against Maryland, dishing the ball over the defender to an open teammate for another score.

This time, Carter is the one passing the ball and dishes it to her open teammate in the paint against Maryland pic.twitter.com/HB86iFiTK0— Noah Aberegg (@aberegg_noah) April 30, 2026

During her time at Georgetown, Carter recorded just 12 blocks on the season. However, that number was nearly doubled in each of her seasons with Butler.

Against the Terrapins, the Indianapolis native jumped out of the lane to play on-ball defense. After the ball was passed to her matchup, she quickly rushed back between her and the basket with her arms up, smacking the ball out of the air.

Carter’s playmaking abilities extend to the defensive side, where she quickly moves back in position and denies the shot pic.twitter.com/YdxI59wnGI— Noah Aberegg (@aberegg_noah) April 30, 2026

Petra Božan

Fortunately for the Lady Lions, general manager and assistant coach Jason Crafton is the only returner who saw Božan’s career-high 17-point performance against Penn State last season.

The Croatia native has a slightly higher-scoring output than Carter, averaging 6.6 points per game. After coming off the bench in all 33 games her freshman year, Bozan saw a bigger role in Year 2, making 19 starts.

Against the blue and white, Bozan played a big role in the team’s only 100-point performance during the Big Ten schedule.

Bozan showcased her 6-foot-3 height right off the bat, jumping high to intercept a pass thrown at 6-foot-6 Gracie Merkle, which normally has no issues reaching her.

Nebraska transfer Petra Bozan earns a steal on the first play of the game against Penn State, intercepting a pass intended for 6-foot-6 Gracie Merkle pic.twitter.com/iMAH53PA3c— Noah Aberegg (@aberegg_noah) April 30, 2026

However, that wasn’t the only success she had against Merkle. After receiving the inbound under the basket, Bozan stepped back out of the paint and scored over Merkle, putting the first points on the board for either team.

Bozan also has offensive success against Merkle, scoring a step-back jumper over her tall frame pic.twitter.com/Vor7KcocPh— Noah Aberegg (@aberegg_noah) April 30, 2026

That physicality and finishing success were also on display later in the season as the Cornhuskers were battling Iowa on the road.

Separated by four points halfway through the final quarter, Bozan grabbed the rebound and converted on a second-chance opportunity, earning an opportunity at the free throw line as well.

Against Iowa, Bozan took full control of the play, securing the rebound, scoring the second-chance layup and earning the and-one opportunity pic.twitter.com/jOTlPp2oao— Noah Aberegg (@aberegg_noah) April 30, 2026

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball hosts No. 2 Nebraska for last regular season series
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is set to face its hardest opponent on the regular season schedule. No. 2 Nebraska will visit Happy Valley to close out the regular season with a Big Ten series.

The Nittany Lions have a current four-game winning streak, sweeping Illinois for their last road conference series, then defeating Saint Francis for their last nonconference opponent of the 2026 season.

This weekend’s meeting will be the 25th time the Cornhuskers and the blue and white will go head-to-head on the diamond in program history. Nebraska leads the overall series 16-8.

Nebraska is on a 15-game win streak, with a 40-6 overall record. The Cornhuskers sit at the first slot of the Big Ten conference, 20-1 in conference play with a 13-game win streak. The last loss Nebraska faced was in late March, losing to then No.7 UCLA in a final 6-5.

Rhonda Revelle is in her 34th season as the Cornhuskers head coach. With over 1,100 wins, Revelle has been the face of the program at her alma mater. Being a part of the inaugural year of the Women's College World Series in 1982, she was also named to the All-Big Eight Selection that same year.

Entering the 2026 season, Revelle owns the third-most wins of any active Division I coach.

Having a roster filled with young talent, Nebraska has three players over .400 batting average.

Right-handed senior pitcher Jordy Frahm is one of the elite in her graduating class. A Papillion, Nebraska, native is finishing her fifth season of eligibility. Winning the WCWS in her first two years at Oklahoma, Frahm decided to finish her college career in her home state.

She has more than thrived in the Cornhuskers uniform; the two-way player has a 1.30 ERA and .423 batting average in her final year. So far this season, she has been able to strike out 12 batters in a single game and has 17 home runs on the season. In 2025, Frahm was named NFCA DI Player of the Year and Big Ten Pitcher and Player of the Year.

The Nittany Lions won’t only see Frahm in the circle, but also left-hander Hannah Camenzind as well. The senior is a Valley, Nebraska, native who is also a dual player with a 1.40 ERA and a .408 batting average on the season.

Outfielder Alina Felix sits in first place in the Cornhuskers' batting average with a .455 in her senior season.

Frahm and Camenzind are both in the top ten of the Big Ten batters, but Frahm currently takes the first spot for pitchers in the conference.

The Nittany Lions roster has an aggressive approach in the batter’s box, with freshman infielder Allison Oneacre at the top of the offense with a .388 batting average and 17 homers in her inaugural season.

On the pitching side of the ball, righty McKenna Young leads the Nittany Lions with a 3.31 ERA, and freshman Mackenzie Duncan has the most strikeouts with 73 on the season.

As this will be the hardest opponent Penn State will face so far in their historic 2026 campaign, this upcoming series looks to be a challenging one for the blue and white before postseason play begins.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: No. 15 Penn State men’s lacrosse tops No. 11 Maryland, advances to Big Ten championship
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 15 Penn State opened conference play against No. 11 Maryland on March 21, and started its postseason with a rematch against the Terrapins in the Big Ten semifinal.

The regular season meeting ended in favor of the Nittany Lions, as the blue and white secured a 10-6 victory in Happy Valley. Panzer Stadium was filled to the brim, with a venue-record 2,301 fans in attendance to watch Penn State’s first-ever home victory over Maryland.

On Thursday night, the familiar foes headed to a neutral site, Rutgers’ SHI Stadium, with a berth in the conference championship on the line. The blue and white grinded out an 8-6 win in a close, defensive battle.

Eric Spanos got the Terrapins on the board first, beating his man off the dodge and cutting down the alley before whipping the ball just over Preston Hawkins’ head and into the Nittany Lion nylon.

Most of the opening quarter consisted of the teams’ traded defensive plays, until the blue and white finally got on the board when Michael Faraone dunked one home with 4:54 left in the frame. The scoreless run marked the longest game-opening drought all season for Penn State.

It wouldn’t take long for it to get another, however, as attackman Hunter Aquino faked his defender out and rolled around the crease to slam a close shot home, putting Penn State up 2-1 late in the frame. He’d get his second towards the midpoint of the second frame, giving Penn State a two-goal advantage.

After scoring the first goal of the game less than three minutes into the opening quarter, Maryland wouldn’t find the back of the net again in the first half. Strong defense on both sides brought the game into the break with the blue and white ahead 3-1.

Penn State’s defensive stubbornness held coach John Tillman’s offense to an uncharacteristically low output, as the lone goal marked Maryland’s lowest first-half total all season.

Early fouls on Jack Iannantuono and Jon King gave the Terrapins a two-man advantage, and attackman Leo Johnson capitalized quickly with a close, far-side shot that beat Hawkins to end the nearly 30 minute scoring drought.

Just after the Maryland score, the Nittany Lions’ defense came up with a stop, and defenseman Will Costin drove upfield with the ball in his stick. A late slide allowed the 6-foot-1 defender to get his hands free, wind up and power his second goal of the season into the net.

On the next Penn State possession, quick passing worked Aquino free, and he fired a lefty shot into the top-right corner of the net to seal his hat trick.

Maryland got one back not long after, when Johnson fired his second goal of the night to bring the Nittany Lion advantage to two goals at the third quarter’s media timeout. The lead would hold until the end of the quarter, with the blue and white up 5-3 as the buzzer sounded.

A close call on the goal line warranted a challenge from Tillman, but the initial call of no goal stood, and the Terrapins lost a timeout. On the ensuing possession, Mac Hobbs drove towards the crease and was pushed into it, putting Penn State man-up in a crucial situation.

As Luke Walstrum worked from X, Liam Matthews settled on the crease. Walstrum caught Maryland ball-watching and fed the Canadian, who flicked it past Terrapin netminder Brian Ruppel.

Despite being down late, Maryland kept at it. Spanos drew a slide and found Jack Schultz, who was wide open for an easy score on a delayed penalty.

The Terrapins got another quick, slick goal two minutes later when Chris Lyons hitched past a Penn State defender and fed Braden Erksa to narrow the lead to just one.

On the consequent faceoff, Ryan O’Connor scooped the ball off the turf and flicked an underhand pass to Jon King, who ran down the throat of the Maryland defense and fired a low shot past Ruppel.

Spanos found the net again when he powered past his defender and whipped a sidearm shot past Hawkins’right leg. The goal brought the score to a 7-6 Nittany Lions lead with 2:40 to go in the game.

With seconds draining off the clock, reigning Big Ten Freshman of the Week Andrew Beard beat his defender and finished on goal to deliver the game-sealing goal. Maryland had a final offensive chance, but a save from Hawkins allowed the blue and white to hang on to their lead until the end.

Up next

With their victory on Thursday, the Nittany Lions advance to Saturday’s Big Ten championship, where they’ll face No. 7 Johns Hopkins. Faceoff is set for noon at SHI Stadium.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: McKenna added to Team Canada roster ahead of World Hockey Championship
author: Luci Palacios | For the Collegian
description:
Gavin McKenna is headed to the international stage, earning a spot on Team Canada’s roster for the 2026 World Hockey Championship.

Gavin McKenna added to Canada’s roster ahead of the 2026 World Hockey Championship in Switzerland on May 15-31!! 🏒🇨🇦Read ➡️ https://t.co/HWQHuiGFyb#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/WW0HcFxw2l— Penn State Men’s Hockey (@PennStateMHKY) April 30, 2026

McKenna gets the call after a strong first season with the Nittany Lions.The White Horse, Yukon, native earned the nod after a record-setting freshman season where he totaled 51 points.

The forward's point total set a Penn State freshman record, and his 36 assists set a single-season program record.

The tournament is set for May 15–31, where 16 teams will compete for the title in Zurich and Fribourg, Switzerland.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball returns home for conference series against Minnesota
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
The Nittany Lions return to Happy Valley after an unsuccessful trip out west. Penn State was unable to earn a victory in the three-game series against then-No. 19 Oregon, ultimately suffering a sweep as the season nears its end. After its game against #18 West Virginia was canceled due to rain, attention now turns to another Big Ten opponent in Minnesota for the blue and white.

With only two home series remaining, upcoming games pose as must-wins for Penn State if it wants to hold a Big Ten tournament spot.

Recent games have favored the Nittany Lions, who are on a three-game win streak against the Gophers, with their last game ending in a high-scoring 15-10 victory. However, both teams have had similar seasons as they currently hold losing conference records.

Pitching troubles seemed to flip for Penn State coming off the series loss to the Ducks. Two out of three starters went six innings and combined for 17 strikeouts. Colin Fitzgerald had a game to remember after recording his career-high 12 punchouts in a game and only allowing three earned runs.

The seemingly rejuvenated pitching staff goes against a Minnesota offense that ranks 10th in the Big Ten. The Golden Gophers’ lineup has been doing very well at the plate as of late, earning over 10 runs in two of their last four games.

Weber Neels leads his team with a .359 batting average and 12 home runs. The Cottage Grove, Minnesota, native has five homers in his last seven games and has been a key contributor in the Gophers five-game win streak.

A hot offense has been backed up by a pitching staff that hasn’t allowed over three runs in its last five games. Minnesota’s pitching currently ranks top-five in the Big Ten with a 4.17 team ERA. Starting pitcher Marcus Kruzan leads the Minnesota starters with a 3.00 ERA. The junior has allowed six earned runs in his last six games while striking out 20 total batters.

Additionally, the Golden Gophers' bullpen has been able to keep wins intact in recent games. Five relievers currently have below a 3.00 ERA heading into the weekend.

However, the pitching staff will face an improving Penn State lineup. Bryce Molinaro used the weekend to continue staying on track in the season. The Drums, Pennsylvania, native hit a homer in his recent series, earning a total of 3 RBIs.

Michael Anderson continues to be a problem for pitchers. Although not recording an RBI over the weekend, the senior still sits tied at first in home runs in the Big Ten, continuing his dominant first season in the blue and white.

Penn State will need its bats to stay hot against a Minnesota team that has been lighting up the scoreboard and can keep an opposing lineup quiet. A series win could boost confidence for the Nittany Lions entering their last seven games of the season.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State picks up commitment from 4-star Stanley Montgomery
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Ikaika Malloe stayed in Pennsylvania for his latest addition to the defensive line in the Class of 2027, picking up a 4-star recruit from Philadelphia.

Stanley Montgomery committed to Penn State on Thursday, he announced via X.

Montgomery is the No. 7 player out of Pennsylvania and No. 24 defensive lineman in his class, per 247Sports composite.

He was offered to be a Nittany Lion on Jan. 15 while already committed to Syracuse, the first school to make him an official offer. However, Montgomery decommitted from the Orange on Feb. 3. He later chose Penn State over Kentucky, Rutgers and Virginia with an official visit to Happy Valley set for June 5.

In his junior season with Archbishop Ryan High School, Montgomery tallied 45 tackles including eight sacks and 19 tackles for loss, totaling 84 yards.

The 6-foot-3, 300-pounder was named First Team All-Catholic for both offensive and defensive line duties, aiding the Raiders in capturing the Philadelphia Catholic League Blue Division football championship for the second year in a row.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball junior Brooke Klosowicz tabbed as top utility player
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State's softball Brooke Klosowicz named a top-five utility player by Softball America

one of the nation’s best🎱#WeAre | #AllIn pic.twitter.com/wCXZgwZijh— Penn State Softball (@PennStateSB) April 30, 2026

The Nittany Lion 2026 squad has done it again, getting their third honor just this week. This time it was veteran junior Brooke Klosowicz.

Softball America announced Thursday afternoon D1 softball's top utility players of this year, with the Johnsburg, Illinois, native getting the fourth slot in the rankings.

So far, Klosowicz has a .357 batting average and 0.881 fielding percentage in her junior season.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball announces Dollar Dog Weekend for upcoming series against Minnesota
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
Dollar Dog Night is set to turn into Dollar Dog Weekend.

🌭Dollar Dog Weekend🌭Lost a few Dollar Dog Nights this year, so let’s have some fun this weekend!#WeAre pic.twitter.com/TxOInBNnbS— Penn State Baseball (@PennStateBASE) April 30, 2026

Over the three-game series against Minnesota this weekend, the first 2,000 hot dogs sold in every game will be only $1.

Penn State was forced to cancel its last Dollar Dog Night of the season against No. 18 West Virginia due to weather on Wednesday.

First pitch for Game 1 against the Gophers will take place at 5:30 p.m. on Friday. The following games begin at 2 p.m. on Saturday and 1 p.m. on Sunday.

title: Senior Column | Time made me bolder
author: Mercedes Hamilton | Editor-in-Chief
description:
In true Mercedes fashion, I’m sitting in the Willard Building past midnight with tears in my eyes and a rapidly approaching deadline. The voices of the people I’ve spent every waking minute with this year slip through in my headphones over Fleetwood Mac’s "Landslide" as I struggle to put the last four years into a few thousand words.

If I’m being honest, I didn’t want to come to Penn State. It was actually the very last school I applied to, and I waited all the way until March to do it.

I grew up in Brooklyn and had my eyes set on a college in the SUNY system or my mom’s alma mater, Syracuse University. But during COVID, we moved to Pennsylvania, and financially, a state school made the most sense.

I don’t care much for sports or parties, and the thought of attending a PWI in the middle of nowhere Pennsylvania scared the crap out of me.

After exploring downtown on admitted students day, my mom and I trudged up Shortlidge Road. The bright blue news box at the bus stop in front of the White Building caught my eye. I flipped through the student newspaper in awe, sweat dripping down my forehead as my mom raved about the beautiful campus.

As an incoming English major who loved to write, I immediately decided I’d apply for The Daily Collegian when I arrived on campus in the fall. At the time, I had no idea just how much this newspaper would change my life.

During my first few months, I wasn’t quite sure if I was going to stay at Penn State. It took me a long time to make friends and find my place. I missed my mom, and I desperately wanted to go home. The only thing I had to look forward to was Monday night lifestyle budget meetings, and hopefully, seeing my byline in the paper on Friday morning.

I kept to myself. I didn’t talk to many people, and I left Willard as soon as I finished my work. Even the newsroom felt a bit overwhelming and isolating at times. And the first few interviews were terrifying — walking up to strangers to chat with me for an article in the paper made me deeply uncomfortable.

But I loved to write, and quickly realized and became inspired by the impact and legacy of our paper. So I picked up as many stories as I could.

By the end of the semester, I was in the Bellisario College of Communications walk-in advising, begging them to override me into COMM 160: Basic News Writing Skills, which you had to be a communications major to enroll in. I added journalism as a second major as soon as I could.

The summer after my freshman year, I was surprised to be selected as news editor. I stuttered during staff meetings and flinched every time I got a Slack ping, hoping I hadn’t made a mistake. And that I did. But eventually, I got into the swing of things. The tight deadlines and constant chaos were exhilarating.

It was then I knew I wanted to work my way up to editor-in-chief.

The Daily Collegian has consumed my entire college experience. I’ve sacrificed outings with my friends, edited stories on the beach during family vacations over the summer, missed performances with my dance teams and admittedly skipped a few classes.

A few days ago, when my best friend from home visited for the first time and I showed her the office, she told me she thought Willard was my dorm because she always sees my little “Find My iPhone” icon there late at night.

Even when I studied abroad, I continued writing for the paper and joined midnight Zoom calls from my Dublin apartment.

From investigating a slew of racist, hateful anonymous emails several professors received to diving into the university’s shameful history with Indigenous peoples, the work I was able to produce has taught me so much about empathy, ethics and accountability.

By the time I began working on my proposal, I had worked across a few Collegian staffs, from news to lifestyle to ops to social media.

And still, nothing could have prepared me for this year as editor-in-chief.

It was a really difficult year.

There were so many challenges I couldn’t have even anticipated, like navigating nearly the school year without a general manager and losing valued staff members for using artificial intelligence to write stories. Growing AI misuse was insanely frustrating and swiftly expanding our policy it became one of the defining parts of my tenure.

The year was relentless. It didn’t matter if I was sick or tired or if I needed a break. I had a job to do and the pressure was immense.

I cried a lot. I didn’t always know if I was capable or if I was doing a good job, and I sometimes regretted applying for the role. I wasn’t always sure if I was making the right decision or if my peers were happy with me. Nasty social media comments and emails from readers weighed on me. Almost nothing went as planned. I lost lots of sleep and missed out on a lot of experiences.

The landslide did indeed bring me down. But nothing worth doing comes easy.

Nothing has made me feel more fulfilled than seeing my very first paper on campus newsstands with my name listed as editor-in-chief on the ops page; watching confetti fall behind my laptop on press row after spending 40 hours on press row at THON; conducting the Collegian's first student survey and editing our first big data story; watching our hard work be recognized regionally and nationally; watching the candidates I trained be selected as summer editors.

Victoria Gough and Sarah Pellis, thank you for encouraging me to apply to be summer news editor. I don’t think you two realize how big that was for me. I would not be writing the column I’m writing right now without that push.

Thank you to Megan Swift, Nick Stonesifer and Amy Schafer for showing me the ropes and being here every step of the way no matter how far away y'all are. Your confidence in me truly went miles. The EICs Anonymous chat will never go away.

Amy, there’s no one I’d rather be trauma-bonded with. You were a great, innovative business manager, and I feel very lucky to have worked alongside you this year. Your drive and ambition is admirable and will take you very far. Thank you for a successful year and for sharing this crazy experience with me.

Thank you to all of my past co-editors, Maddie Seeling, Ella Castronuovo, Kaitlyn Murphy and Daniela White.

Thank you to the very talented Jadzia Santiago, who was one of my very first friends at the Collegian and at Penn State. There’s no one I’d rather yap about poetry with. And to Milan Varia, who was also in my very first candidate class and has become a great friend of mine. Thank you both for listening and for sharing.

Maggie Messitt, thank you for letting me cry in your office and for making sure I didn’t eat party mix for dinner every day. Walter Middlebrook, thank you for your wisdom, guidance and for many laughs. Gary Abdullah, thank you for listening to me rant about the Collegian and still supporting me when I ran for editor-in-chief.

Molly, Tony, Sarah, Adam, Rick, Dave and Laurie, thank you all for your help and support this year. All of us students in Willard 202 appreciate you very much.

Thank you to my girls on the Luxurious Lionesses and my teammates on BLK Elites for getting me out of the office and offering a space to decompress and have fun. I would have gone insane otherwise. I will miss dancing with you all deeply.

Thank you, Trinity for being the most constant thing about my time at Penn State. And Kamya, for being a great friend. There's no one else I would have rather spent my senior year with.

The biggest thank you to Penn State’s English department for remaining a space for me to explore my passion for reading and writing in the midst of a very time consuming experience as a student journalist. I have learned so much about myself and about the world through my literature and creative writing courses. I will never forget the novels and poems that drew me to writing in the first place.

Thank you, Matt Tierney and Julia Kasdorf for your patience and support and for allowing me to start an entirely new thesis in the middle of the spring semester (this is a story for another day). I appreciate how you’ve trusted, believed in and supported me throughout my time at Penn State.

Geraldine, Victoria, Mia and Tyler and Rachael, thank you for the long nights in the office, the laughs and constant reassurance. Your futures are very bright, and I can’t wait to see what you all do at the Collegian and beyond from afar.

Avery Tortora and Kahlie Wray, thank you for being my managing editors this year. When I asked you two to apply, you had no idea what was ahead. Still, you dove in head first and thrived. I am endlessly impressed with y’all and appreciate your hard work and support more than you know.

Avery, you were the best digital managing editor I could have asked for. You’re so bright. It’s truly been a privilege to work with you and watch you grow. I can’t wait to see how far you go and am always here.

Kahlie, there is absolutely no one I’d rather be in Willard until 5 a.m. with. Your friendship and spontaneity made my last year at the Collegian something I will cherish always. I’m so proud of how much you’ve grown this year and can’t wait to see what you accomplish as editor-in-chief. Trust yourself. There is no one more capable. Your creativity, talent and positive attitude will take you far. I am in your corner always.

Avery Hill, I had no idea our time as summer editors would have blossomed into the relationship we have today, but I’m so grateful it did. Your drive and character inspires me every day, and I am so proud of everything you've accomplished this year. Thank you for always being here for me. I couldn’t have gotten through the year without you. I’m excited to see what the future holds for us.

Mommy, thank you for molding me into the woman I am today. None of this is possible without your resilience, guidance and support. I love you very much and owe all of this to you.

Thank you to every source who had a story they were willing to share with me. I will never forget the stories you have allowed me to tell, and the journalist it has made me today.

To every single person who has been part of the Collegian this year, thank you. I have learned so much from you. Whether you were a candidate, reporter or editor, I deeply appreciate your hard work and patience and am indescribably proud of what we’ve accomplished this year. None of this is possible without you all.

I can’t thank the Collegian enough for the people it’s brought me and the confidence it has given me. Freshman year me would be extremely proud of the young woman and the journalist I have become. I am forever indebted to this outlet and will carry these experiences far into my career.

I didn’t necessarily get the college experience I wanted. But I wouldn’t have known The Daily Collegian without it.

And for that, I thank you, Dear Old State.

Mercedes Hamilton is a senior majoring in English and journalism. She was The Daily Collegian’s 2025-26 editor-in-chief until 6:15 a.m. Thursday morning.

MORE SENIOR COLUMNS

title: Senior Column | Everything after this is off the record
author: Amy Schafer | Business Manager
description:
Correction: In a previous version of Amy’s life plan she thought she would major in political science and become a lawyer. She has since found her passion for journalism and owes it all to The Daily Collegian.

I love The Daily Collegian.

No amount of journalism training could prepare me for how to write my final column and say goodbye to the place that has been my home for the past four years. I’ll always cherish the memories I made here, the people I’ve met and the stories I’ve written.

When I first arrived at Penn State, I had no plans to become a journalist. I had never written for my high school paper and I wasn’t a journalism major. I learned about the Collegian my first day on Penn State’s campus through former Editor-in-Chief Megan Swift, who happened to be my mentor during Schreyer New Student Orientation.

This, along with an intense interest in Penn State football, was what initially drew me to apply.

After being devastated I didn’t get student football tickets my freshman year, I thought I’d join the student newspaper instead and cover the football team day one. Unfortunately for my freshman self, that’s not how the Collegian works. Luckily, I found something even better than access to the football field — a passion for journalism.

I applied to the sports staff, but ended up getting placed on the news staff as a candidate. A wise woman on staff named Teagan Mayr convinced me that I should give it a try, and that I was going to love news reporting.

As usual, Teagan was right.

My first ever breaking news coverage for the Collegian was a “politically provocative” comedy night Uncensored America hosted featuring Proud Boys founder Gavin McInnes and comedian Alex Stein.

I was originally assigned to cover the speakers, but when the protest turned violent, I learned to be flexible. I worked with the other reporters to pivot and cover the protest instead, producing a triple byline coverage. One of the Collegian photographers at the protest got pepper-sprayed in the face and kept taking photos.

After witnessing this, there was no doubt in my mind that news was the staff I was meant to be placed on. I was insanely impressed by the dedication everyone had to the work they do and knew this was going to be somewhere I felt at home.

I worked for the Collegian as a borough reporter, abroad columnist, member of the social media team, newsletter staff and eventually decided to apply for news editor.

My semester as news editor made me fall even more in love with student journalism, and it led me to meet two of my favorite people in the world: my news editing partner Ella Castronuovo and former investigations editor Anjelica Rubin.

I absolutely loved the opportunity to lead the news staff at the Collegian, and I learned so much from it. I led important coverage, but I also learned how to grow from my mistakes — like posting a breaking news update with a headline that read The Waffle Shop “allegedly” caught fire alongside a photo of it actively in flames.

When I sat down for my end-of-semester meeting in the fall of 2024 with former editor-in-chief Nick and managing editors Olivia and Sophia, I knew I eventually wanted to run for executive leadership.

I’m forever grateful to Nick for having enough confidence in 19-year-old Amy to encourage her to run for editor-in-chief, and say “why not this year” when I expressed an interest in potentially running for EIC my senior year.

Serving as editor-in-chief was the highlight of my time at The Daily Collegian. Although it required many sleepless nights and personal sacrifices, it was such a rewarding and exciting experience. I learned so much about how to be a better journalist and person and how to navigate complex issues.

I started my term as EIC in an unconventional manner — I was thousands of miles away from State College in Buenos Aires, Argentina during my semester abroad. At 19, I was also the youngest EIC that I know of to take on the job. I was filled with a mix of fear and excitement, but I dove in headfirst and poured my whole heart into it.

Although my first few months of EIC were chaotic, I always put the Collegian before anything else, because I cared about it so much. It was always on my mind, whether it be taking a zoom call to plan coverage while hiking Patagonia, meeting with editors mid snowstorm in the southern-most city in the world or taking grant calls mid work day during my internship at an immigration law firm (shoutout to my boss Diego for letting me do this).

Upon returning to State College, I was busy with tons of exciting and difficult news to cover. I learned so much, made difficult decisions and led projects and coverage that had a tangible impact on campus.

I got to lead election coverage in a swing state where Penn State was a key area for many campaigns. We saw tons of political figures come to campus, including AOC, Charlie Kirk and even Donald Trump.

I also launched a Title IX Reporting Lab to investigate issues related to sexual assault, women’s sports and pay equity and got to present the project at Harvard’s Christopher J. Georges Conference on College Journalism.

I strengthened my ability to collaborate across newsrooms and mediums through Penn State Student Media Network’s hazing project. The Collegian collaborated with four other student news outlets at Penn State to produce an investigative project focused on hazing on campus that exposed multiple incidents of hazing and led to sanctions on one group identified in our reporting.

There’s so many other projects that I loved working on including: starting the Collegian’s first ever DEI Committee, collaborating with influencers and participating in the American Press Institute’s Influencer Learning Cohort, launching two new newsletters, expanding our short-form video social media coverage and more.

For every project or success I had, I also faced challenges. Each challenge taught me a lesson.

In November of 2024, Penn State also censored the paper when it removed all of the Collegian’s 35 indoor newsstands from campus. As EIC, I had to stand up for independent student journalism, mobilize coverage and advocate for the importance of free and independent student journalism. This moment deepened my appreciation for freedom of the press and inspired me to do my honors thesis research on how press censorship functions as a form of structural violence.

Student leadership also had to navigate AI use in the newsroom. AI is rapidly shaping the journalism industry and working across the staff to develop policies for our newsroom and continue to improve them was challenging, yet crucial.

Some challenges couldn’t have been anticipated — such as spending almost the entirety of the past year without a general manager. In the absence of a GM, I had to learn how to manage tasks and solve problems typically outside of the scope of the EIC or BM’s responsibilities.

I also witnessed how declining advertising revenue in the newspaper industry in combination with the Collegian losing university funding impacted the newsroom.

I was scared for the Collegian’s future. What if one day the outlet I had come to love and care about so much couldn’t financially sustain itself anymore? The thought terrified me and also inspired me to take on a more active role in making sure the Collegian would be around for decades to come.

I’m unapologetically obsessed with this outlet, and I was going to do everything in my power to make sure the Collegian had a bright future.

So, I decided to run for business manager.

To my knowledge, no one had ever served as both editor-in-chief and business manager. Was I crazy? Yes, but that turned out to be a good thing. The opportunity to develop a deep understanding of non-profit news operations, write grants, fundraise, spearhead projects, develop our audience and more has been such a valuable experience.

I’ve recognized how important it is to understand a product to be able to successfully market it, experimented with innovative ways to grow our audience and learned how communicating the impact of our work can drive donations.

Every step of my Collegian journey has been so formative on the person I am today.

The heart of the work we do here at the Collegian is the people. We’ve talked, fought, laughed, cried and everything in between.

Seeing how much every single person on our staff cares about this outlet inspires me every day.

There’s truly no place like The Daily Collegian. Everyone cares so much about this place and the work we do. I’ve spent countless nights with people from so many different areas of study and backgrounds — all working together to keep the Penn State community informed and produce a product we all care so much about making the best it can be.

First, to my business staff this year, thank you. It’s been so wonderful to get to know each and every one of you, especially everyone who has led alongside me as directors.

To everyone I have worked with on the board of editors both as news editor and EIC, thank you for pushing me and motivating me to do my best work each and every day. So many of you have given so much to this outlet and I am so grateful to have worked with you all.

To the past Collegian leadership — Megan, Nick, Olivia, Sophia, Luke and more — who paved the path for me and inspired me to fall in love with journalism, thank you. Nick and Olivia (my Collegian mom and dad), I’m especially grateful to you. Thank you for making me feel at home on the news staff and teaching me how to be a journalist and leader.

Tony, Maggie, Sarah, Walter, Adam, everyone else who has served on the Collegian board and all the journalism faculty and staff (especially Mila and Shaheen) who have supported me, I appreciate you so much.

Zack, it was so wonderful working with you as business manager. Thank you for encouraging me to run for business manager after I served as editor-in-chief and I’m so grateful I had the chance to work with you over the years.

Mercedes, it’s been so wonderful to work alongside you leading the Collegian this year. Seeing how passionate and brilliant you are has consistently inspired me to continue working hard. We’ve been in leadership together at a challenging and transitional time and there’s no one I would have rather faced everything with. Your drive and commitment to the Collegian and to student journalism is truly unmatched.

Sam and Alex, we went through so much together. Thanks for your drive and hard work as managing editors and listening to my constant 24/7 yapping about all things Collegian. I couldn’t have survived my year as EIC without both of you and will look back on all the late nights we spent in the office together fondly.

Dwight, thank you for changing my perspective on Popeyes and for your constant positivity in the office.

Dan, you once said, “people walk past Peter Parker everyday looking for Spider Man.” I’m glad I didn’t walk past our friendship. I appreciate your dedication to the Collegian and your constant positive attitude about everything more than you know.

Chris Burnis and Tim, thanks for making fundraising for the Collegian so much fun. I appreciate your hard work on all the grants and campaigns we worked on together.

Cearra and Ritamarie it was such a pleasure working with both of you. You both bring such a kind and warm energy to everything you do.

Kaitlyn Murphy, ohhhhhhh boy oh boy oh boy. It’s been so fun working with you over the years. You’re so talented and hard working and I’m so glad we became friends.

Maggie and Teagan, thank you for always being there for me and allowing me to talk about the Collegian 24/7 in the household #boalsburgforever. I’m so blessed to have such kind and supportive friends, I love you both so much.

To Kathryn, my favorite Collegian multimedia editor (sorry Dan and Alex), thank you for everything. From freshman orientation, studying abroad in the Czech Republic, editing together at the Collegian, countless late nights working on our theses together and now being roommates, you’ve been there by my side through it all. I’m so grateful for our friendship.

Jackson, I respect the drive and passion you have for your work as a photojournalist so much. From meeting you at my first Collegian coverage to now, you’ve been such a positive part of my life and Collegian journey. I’m extremely grateful for you constantly supporting and believing in me. No one understands my obsession with the Collegian like you do.

Anjelica and Ella, I am so glad the Collegian brought us together. You have both supported me so much through everything in the Collegian and outside of it. Thanks for letting me practice my EIC proposal to you for literally months and for always believing in me even when I don’t believe in myself. You always know how to make me laugh and be there for me when I need support. You’re both such incredible journalists and I look up to you so much.

Joe Tuman, I couldn’t ask for a better friend — you’re the best. Thanks for being by my side to grow the Collegian’s social media and for letting me talk through all my ideas for the Collegian – good and bad. I am so happy I always have you in my corner.

To everyone else who has shaped my time at the Collegian — Abby, Leo, Ayden, Arthur, Yusheng, Jon, Avery, Tyler, Chris Eutsler, Zach Karpf, all my candidate classes and so many more — thank you.

Mom, dad and the rest of my family, thank you for your continued support of me during college and my Collegian journey. Thank you mom for reading every single article I write. You have always been there to support me in so many ways and I am so grateful for you.

Kahlie, Rhea, Jake, Geraldine and Mia — I know the Collegian’s going to be in great hands next year and I can’t wait to see what you all accomplish.

I’ve learned so much from this outlet, not only about journalism but about myself. I have spent more time in the Willard Building and Collegian office than my own home. Being at the helm of this outlet first as a borough reporter, then news editor, editor-in-chief and now business manager has challenged me in ways that have made me grow into the person I am today.

I will forever hold a special place in my heart for The Daily Collegian. Thank you for everything.

Amy Schafer is a senior majoring in public relations, global and international studies and women’s, gender and sexuality studies and minoring in Spanish. She was The Daily Collegian’s 2025-26 business manager until 6:15 a.m. Thursday morning.

title: Senior Column | I Said Yes And It Changed My Life
author: Zack O'Brien | Vice President
description:
There’s a simple piece of advice that I didn’t fully understand when I first got to this campus: say yes. At the time, I thought it was so cliche to say. But looking back now, I can honestly say that the moments that defined my time here all started with one thing; saying yes.

When I first arrived, I was comfortable staying in what I knew. It’s so easy to just stick to the familiar; routines, people, expectations. Saying no is safe. But what I didn’t realize then is that saying no also limits how much you experience, how much you grow, and ultimately who you become.

Some of the most important opportunities I had at Penn State started as situations I wasn't fully ready for:

“Zack, do you want to apply for Vice President of the Business Division?”

“Yes.”

“Zack, do you want to take over as Business Manager of The Collegian?”

“Yes.”

“Zack, do you accept your full time position offer?”

“Yes.”

See, these are all moments I just said yes, and they ultimately compounded on top of each other. There were times I questioned whether I was qualified, whether I could do it, or could handle the situation. But every time I said yes anyway, I grew into those opportunities. That's the thing about saying yes, it doesn’t mean you're necessarily prepared, it means you're willing.

These all built on top of each other, leading me to find myself doing things I never could have imagined. Leading the Business Division, joining a fraternity, going to Penn State. None of that would have happened if I had waited until I felt “ready.”

It’s not just about the major moments either. Saying yes to spontaneous plans, late night hang outs, I mean even going to the gaff on a Thursday night. Those are just some of the moments that turned into core memories of mine, the ones you don’t realize are important until they are.

Now, as I get ready to leave Penn State, I realize that saying yes is bigger than just a college mindset, it's something that will shape the rest of my path down what we call life. There will always be reasons to say no. To play it safe, avoid a risk, to stay comfortable. But if there is anything Penn State has taught me, it’s that the best opportunities usually come disguised as things that feel uncertain.

Saying yes opened so many doors for me. It led me to so many great opportunities, my best friends, and genuinely a completely different version of myself.

I sometimes feel uncertain about my future, but I'm honestly okay with that. Because I know whatever comes my way, I'll approach them the same way I did here, being willing to say yes.

And if I've learned anything from my time here at this university, it’s that some of the best things in life start with just a simple decision to go for it.

MORE COLUMNS

title: I ‘java’ have it | Reviewing coffee based-cocktails in downtown State College
author: Nadya Solometo | Lifestyle Editor
description:
If you know me, then you might understand the immense infatuation I have for coffee.

Any type of brew: lattes, mochas or even a simple black coffee is liquid gold to me. When going out in downtown State College, I try to find any kind of it.

With there being a multitude of bars for those 21 and over to go to, there’s usually a spot for anyone, whether you’re looking for a sit-down, dancey or dive-bar vibe.

Some of these places offer espresso or coffee-based cocktails and, with my proficiency in all things coffee — I once had 16 coffee cocktails during a single bar crawl — here’s my review on some of the most popular, sure to make you bounce off the walls from joy and too much caffeine.

Doggie’s Pub Iced Coffee

This drink was my first introduction to coffee spiked with a bit more than just an extra shot of espresso.

The cocktail is made with whiskey, coffee liqueur, half and half and your choice of either peanut butter, vanilla or whipped flavored alcohol. I chose peanut butter, which uses Skrewball Peanut Butter Whiskey.

Not only is Doggie’s iced coffee delicious, but it goes down way too smoothly. If you’re not paying attention, you can easily take down four of these in less than an hour, coming from experience.

Rating: 10/10

Phyrst Espresso Martini shots

While Phyrst has another infamous espresso-based shot on their menu, this shot is a less messy version, without the whipped cream.

The Espresso Martini shots are just the right amount of liquor and espresso, making neither taste stronger than the other. Only being $4, these can go down like water, so be careful when trying to have a chill night out.

Rating: 9/10

Champs Espresso Martini

Known for its buckets and dirty sprites, Champs unfortunately fumbles with their espresso martini.

This is the only item on this list I felt most likely came from a can rather than being made fresh. This martini was too watery, and when you do catch a hint of espresso, it was masked by a very powerful wave of vodka.

The Champs’ espresso martini also had a weird syrup consistency once you made it to the bottom of the glass. While I did finish this drink — I’m not made of money, I’m still a college student — this drink was barely enjoyable from the first to last gulp.

Rating: 3/10

Chumley’s Espresso Martini

If you’re looking for a classic, real cocktail, Chumley’s is the place to go. Arguably, this spot serves the best espresso martini you can get downtown.

Since the crowd at Chumley’s is less college-aged, you can tell they care more about what they’re serving their customers. This drink carried a rich taste, making it clear the espresso was freshly pulled.

However, while being an adult bar, the prices follow suit, as this drink was a little bit costly compared to the other options. The crowd being less student-based also makes the spot one to visit if you’re looking for a classier night instead of one where you wind up losing your wallet and memory.

Rating: 8/10

Bill Pickle’s Tap Room Kentucky Frozen Coffee

With any good list, you have to save the best for last.

This is the crème de la crème of coffee-based drinks in downtown State College, and honestly in the nearest 50 miles.

Pickle’s Kentucky Frozen Coffee has more of a milkshake consistency, and tastes just like one. This item boasts Kentucky whiskey, ground coffee beans, vanilla ice cream and Rothrock coffee, all of which swirl together in the machine and become heavenly.

This drink is priced similarly to Doggie’s at $6 and, while a bit smaller than its competition, still stands next to it in taste.

You can have this drink any time of year or on any kind of night, whether country themed or not.

I’ve had so much of this drink, one of the bartenders gave me three of its specialty cups to have as a keepsake for when I graduate in a few weeks. What he didn’t realize however, is that I would’ve much more appreciated taking the machine they make these in — fully filled too.

Rating: 100/10
