Through my experiences, I've realized that I do have some mantras I use to push through even the most grueling insanity in college. Here are some of the ones that helped me, whether it be with a press pass or not.

Tip 1: Love what you do. If your heart truly is not intimate with it, it may not be for you.

Tip 2: Always push for access, even if you don’t think you will get it. Growing up, my parents always told me to ask again or ask someone else after being rejected, because you never know what’ll happen the second time.

My junior year, I got turned down by every equipment desk on campus for renting a camera, until I asked Professor John Pendygraft after my first photo night, an event the photojournalism community has to get together, bond and learn. After hearing my story, he helped me in many ways to create my first photojournalistic portfolio, which allowed me to join the Collegian.

Tip 3: Always ask questions; too many are better than none. My first photo editors, Kate (Photo Goat) Hildebrand and Chris (Big Dawg) Eutsler, know this better than anyone. Even if it’s a small question that you think is inconsequential, such as reviewing a caption that you’ve written a million times before. In the long run, ensuring you follow the proper procedure is better than getting fired.

Tip 4: Don’t stay in your own comfort zone; get out of your lane. At the end of my junior year, I had decided to create a cooking column, which was insanely hard for someone who sucks at writing. Luckily, thanks to fate, I was able to meet Professor Walter Middlebrook, previously my uncle’s editor at Newsday, who ended up becoming what I think my uncle would have been had he been alive.

I say this with utmost honesty, but since my initial meeting with him, I have never seen so much red pen ink on paper. But thanks to him, “Klein’s Kitchen” turned out to be something I sincerely enjoyed creating both as a photographer and now as a writer.

Tip 5: Play and have fun! This is a lesson I learned in a high school film class, but it has shaped the way I approach everything. There’s no improvement without experimentation, but there is no experimentation without playing. The chocolate lava cake was created by a chef who was playing with chocolate sponge cakes, and both of the awards I have won in the past four years have been the result of projects that I played with nonstop.

Tip 6: The only person you need to compete with is yourself. I spent the past two years looking at photographers like Alex (The Wizard) Antoniono, Nia (Master Class) Meyers and Esteban (The Goat) Marenco, whom I had only dreamed of being as good as. After spending time with the three of them during the Mountain Workshops in Kentucky, we had created friendly nicknames for one another. I realized that there’s no need for competition with others. The only individual who holds you back from being as good as they are is the past version of you.

Tip 7: Don’t let others shape you. Many people over the years have argued that I may not be cut out for photojournalism and that I should consider different careers. I took the criticisms and threw them back in their faces. People hate because they don’t want you to succeed, but when you do succeed, they don’t have room to hate.

Final Tip: Put your head down and run. My favorite comic superhero was Barry Allen’s Flash. I loved him specifically for one reason: He puts his head down and gives everything he has into whatever he is doing. I tend to come off as very vibrant and fast-paced, especially since I speak with a New Yorker’s pace. But, if you put 100% into everything you do, there’s nothing you can’t do, even if you tear your labrum!

Anyways, the actual point of making my way through college after switching majors went really well, and there were no real hassles in making my way from Point A to Point B because my siblings, Bubby and Alexis, as well as my parents, had my back through the entire ride.

To Zach Karpf, Caleb Craig, Sammy Oropeza, Aayan Hamdani, Alex Achmiz, Dustin Danckwerth, Madelyn Collelo, Rylee Weaver, Craig Dixon, William Yeh, Young Nam, Caleb Soi, everyone I have mentioned previously, as well as the many staffers I’ve had the pleasure to sit with at “The Table.”

And finally, to The Daily Collegian.

Thank you for allowing me to realize my end-of-sophomore-year switch to photojournalism was the best thing that’s ever happened to me.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Penn State track and field competes at historic Penn Relays
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
The outdoor track and field season is winding down with just one meet left of the regular season. Despite this, Penn State continued to set top marks to close out the season.

The Nittany Lions competed at the historic Penn Relays in Philadelphia from Thursday to Saturday, earning two first-place finishes and two top-10 marks in school history.

Sophomore Gabby Cope opened the event with a personal best in the college women’s hammer throw, recording a mark of 56.31 meters for second place.

While junior Gabi Deglau didn’t earn a personal-best, she threw a season-best 55.30 meters for fifth place, followed by Alex Pancoast’s 54.16 meters for seventh place.

The Nittany Lions also earned a big finish in the college men’s hammer throw championship as graduate thrower Collin Burkhart threw 67.15 meters for third overall.

The first track event for Penn State was the women’s 1,500-meters, where sophomore Ada Rand ran a 4:17.11 to finish 13th overall. Senior Lea Hatcher finished just behind in 4:17.11 for 16th place.

Penn State’s first victory of the day came from junior Nick Sloff, who ran 3:40.85 in the men’s college 1,500-meter championship.

The Nittany Lions continued to wow in the college men’s 3,000-meter steeplechase championship as senior Ryan Watson ran the fastest time in program history since 1988. Watson ran 8:46.52 to finish fifth, marking the fifth-best time in Penn State history. Junior Logan Strawser ran a personal-best 8:54.63 for 18th place.

Four Nittany Lions took the track for the college women’s 5,000-meter championship, with senior Katie Dallas leading the way with a personal-best 16:18.42 for 22nd place.

Friday

Junior Joe White kicked off the Penn Relays on Friday in the college men’s long jump, where he finished eighth with a mark of 6.99 meters.

In the college men’s discus throw, MIT transfer Sam Engebretson earned a top-five finish with a distance of 51.37 meters, a season best. That top-five Penn State finish carried over into the college men’s pole vault, as sophomore Mason Bucks jumped 4.87 meters for fifth place.

The first of Penn State’s top performances came in the college men’s 4x400-meter championship of America prelims, where the relay of Carter Fitzgerald, Peyton Sommers, Matt DeMatteo and Jake Palermo ran 3:05.39 for fifth place, a spot in Saturday’s finals and the 10th-best time in school history.

The Nittany Lions took that a step further in the College Women’s Sprint Medley Championship of America, as Addyson White, Alexandria North, Marie-Eloise Leclair and Hayley Kitching broke the school record with a time of 3:42.35, winning the race.

Saturday

Penn State kicked off the final day of the Penn Relays in the college men’s high jump championship as freshman Trip Campbell earned a third-place finish with a mark of 2.14 meters, followed by Robert Allen in 10th place with 2.01 meters.

In the college men’s pole vault championship, senior Tristan McGarrah improved his No. 5 mark in school history, jumping 5.17 meters for fifth place overall.

Freshman Skye Petrie-Cameron earned a personal best in the college women’s shot put throw championship, recording a fifth-place mark of 15.90 meters. Sasha Garnett threw 15.73 meters for seventh place.

The first of Penn State’s big moments came in the college men’s 4xMile championship of America, where the relay of Ryan Watson, Nick Sloff, Brian DiCola and Justin Healey ran 16:52.08 for eighth place and the school record.

In the college women’s javelin throw championship, Audrey Friedman finished in sixth place with a throw of 45.81 meters.

The Nittany Lions’ record-breaking day continued in the college women’s 4x800-meters championship of America, as the relay of Allison Johnson, Tayissa Buchanan, Boh Ritchie and Hayley Kitching broke the school record with a first-place finish in 8:18.43. Kitching’s final lap of 2:01.96 solidified the victory for Penn State.

While Penn State didn’t win the college men’s 4x800-meters championship of America, it still broke the school record in 7:10.00. Yukichi Ishii, Andrew Regnier, Niko Schultz and Allon Clay finished second, losing by less than one second.

Three Nittany Lions finished in the top 10 of the college men’s shot put championship, led by Dylan Lambrecht in first place with 19.34 meters. Ryan Henry finished second with 18.91 meters, followed by freshman Bryce Thompson in sixth with 18.02 meters.

Penn State wrapped up the day in the college men’s javelin throw championship, where freshman Nolan Carey threw 65.89 meters for fourth place.

Up next

Penn State wraps up the outdoor regular season with the Larry Ellis Invitational on May 1 and 2.

MORE TRACK COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s volleyball takes down Charleston to advance to EIVA Championship
author: Chase Mitchell | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has been patiently waiting to see who it would face in the EIVA Semifinals after earning the top seed in the tournament and the first-round bye.

The Nittany Lions’ mystery opponent was revealed to be Charleston after the Golden Eagles defeated Sacred Heart. Mark Pavlik and the blue and white won both matches against Charleston 3-0 back in the first weekend of April.

In the end, Penn State came out on top, winning 3-2 in dramatic five-set fashion to advance to the EIVA Championship match.

Charleston got on the board first in the opening set due to a Matthew Luoma attack error that was followed by a Sean Harvey kill, which tied it up for the blue and white.

Harvey ended the season with the third-most kills in the EIVA and the best hitting percentage, at .324%.

Penn State stayed on top for the following several minutes, jumping out to a 6-3 lead backed by kills from Luoma, Bennett Wilson and Gaige Gabriel.

Wilson got back in on the action, notching a service ace to give the blue and white a two-point lead after the Golden Eagles went on a 4-0 run that tightened the score.

A flashy series paid off for Charleston with a block assist going to Yotam Briger and Pavlin Ivanov and an attack error going to Wilson as the Golden Eagles got out to a 15-14 lead.

Another 3-0 scoring run for Charleston extended its lead to 18-15 and forced the Nittany Lions into a timeout after back-to-back Lucas Torres kills.

Torres finished the regular season with the eighth-most kills in the conference, with 193 total.

The Golden Eagles captured the first set thanks to a Harvey attack error and moved one set closer to upsetting the blue and white and crushing its EIVA Championship hopes.

Again, Charleston opened up the scoring in the second frame with a Max Nissen kill before Harvey matched it with a kill of his own to tie the action.

Contrary to the opening set, the second was all Charleston in the early running as the Golden Eagles jumped out to a 7-3 lead backed by a pair of Owen Rose errors, another kill for Nissen and a block assist that went down as a Tristan Hassell attack error.

Penn State turned on the jets, using a 6-1 scoring run to even the second set at 9-9 before Charleston used its first timeout of the night.

In their efforts to cut into the deficit, the Nittany Lions saw Hassell, Harvey and Gaige each notch another kill while Wilson recorded two service aces.

The Nittany Lions grabbed what felt like their first lead in a long time after a Rose service error followed by a Hassell and Gaige block assist score, forcing the Golden Eagles into another timeout as the scoreboard read 17-13 in the blue and white’s favor.

Charleston made things interesting as the action was coming to a head, but it wasn’t enough and Penn State took the second set 25-19 on a Golden Eagle service error.

The blue and white got out to an early 5-1 lead in the third set, pushing Charleston into another timeout after two Harvey kills that moved him up to nine on the night.

Not much changed as Penn State jumped up to a monstrous 13-5 lead after another Hassell kill that came out like a missile just before the Golden Eagles took another timeout.

Back-to-back Charleston kills from Torres and Kai Cousins and a Hassell attack error cut Penn State’s lead to 16-10, but momentum was still very much on the Nittany Lions’ side.

Consecutive Samuel Alo attack errors didn’t help his squad’s cause, and the blue and white’s lead grew to 20-12 with the end of the third set in sight.

Penn State cruised to an easy third-set victory at the hands of a Rose kill that was the middle blocker’s sixth of the night.

With just one set to go for the Nittany Lions to advance and compete for an EIVA Championship, it was the Golden Eagles who jumped out to an early 2-0 lead in the fourth frame, but back-to-back kills from Rose and Harvey tied the action at 2-2.

A 6-2 scoring run for Charleston that saw four errors extended the Golden Eagles’ lead to 8-4 in the early running of the fourth set, as Penn State seemingly had no answer.

Harvey’s serve was just off the mark and resulted in a point for Charleston, which made its lead 15-11 and served as the 12th error of the set.

Edging closer to a fourth set victory, the Golden Eagles’ lead was extended to 20-18 thanks to another Torres kill that sucked all the wind out of Penn State’s sails as it used a timeout.

Some points and a timeout later, it was Charleston that captured the fourth set 25-22 after another Torres kill, meaning an extra period of volleyball was needed to determine which program would advance.

In the abbreviated fifth period, Charleston again got on the board first by way of a Wilson attack error, but the mistake was made up for by a Hassell kill – his 15th of the night.

The Golden Eagles had the early momentum, but a 4-1 blue and white scoring run gave Pavlik’s squad the narrow 5-3 advantage.

Things got a little bit chippy, but momentum stuck with the Nittany Lions the rest of the way as they sailed to a 15-8 victory in the ultimate set, with a Rose kill putting a cap on the night to send his squad to the Conference Final.

Up Next

Penn State will face Princeton in the EIVA Championship match inside Rec Hall on Saturday at 7 p.m.

The Tigers finished the regular season with an overall record of 8-14, but snuck into the EIVA Tournament and got hot, taking down three-seed George Mason in the opening round and two-seed NJIT in a semifinals round match that went to the fifth set.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State OL Vega Ioane drafted 14th overall by Baltimore Ravens
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has now produced a first-round pick in three straight years.

Vega Ioane was selected 14th overall by the Baltimore Ravens in the first round of the 2026 NFL Draft. He was the fourth offensive lineman off the board.

Ioane’s draft stock soared after back-to-back stellar seasons as the Nittany Lions’ starting left guard. Across over 1,000 snaps the last two years, Ioane didn’t allow a single sack after allowing three in 2023.

He really caught the nation’s eye, however, against UCLA in 2024 after destroying defensive end Luke Schuermann while lined up out wide. That highlight showed not only Ioane’s strength, but his versatility as an offensive lineman.

“There’s not many guys that are 6-foot-4 and 350 pounds that can move like him,” current Indianapolis Colt and former Nittany Lion Tyler Warren said after that win. “Seeing a big guy that gets to run around and hit people like that is always going to put a little spark in the sideline and our offense.”

Ioane went viral once again a year later, but this time for something he did off the field. After losing to Ohio State amidst a tough season, Ioane saw an equipment manager struggling to push a cart up the tunnel ramp, so he jumped over the railing to help him.

The video, which was originally posted by Mark Brennan of 247Sports, garnered over 111,000 likes on X and was posted by numerous other organizations.

“Simple as one of my teammates, just a different version,” Ioane said of the moment in early November. “A lot of people, who are outside the building, don't know that we don't run without them guys like that. We can't practice without them. We can't go to games without them. Just as simple as helping out our teammate.”

The Ravens surely liked seeing their first round pick display that kind of character, but Ioane’s stats and accolades are what truly made him worthy of the early selection.

Ioane earned All-America honors from numerous outlets after the 2025 season despite playing for a 7-6 Penn State squad and was a First Team All-Big Ten selection by the media. He was also a finalist for the Polynesian Player of the Year Award.

After his stellar redshirt junior season, Ioane decided to forego his final year of eligibility and declare for the NFL draft. He participated in the NFL Scouting Combine and Penn State Pro Day, where he impressed scouts and reaffirmed his draft stock.

“It feels good,” Ioane said at pro day. “It's a feeling that I know I put a lot of work into it, knowing that it's all coming together, all connecting, things like that. But knowing that I put my best foot forward to put myself in a position like that, it definitely feels good.”

Ioane is the second Nittany Lions’ offensive lineman to be taken in the first round in the last three years, joining 2024 No. 11 overall pick Olu Fashanu. He is the 58th offensive lineman to be drafted from Penn State.

title: Penn State softball infielder Allison Oneacre named NFCA National Freshman of the Year top 25 finalist
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
As the regular season winds down, accolades are starting to creep around the corner for the Nittany Lions.

Standout freshman infielder Allison Oneacre was named as a top-25 finalist for NFCA's Division I National Freshman of the Year award.

all eyes on AO.#WeAre | #AllIn pic.twitter.com/BEngPkvoUM— Penn State Softball (@PennStateSB) April 23, 2026

The Alpharetta, Georgia, native has been a big bat for the blue and white this season, leading the team with a .385 batting average, 16 home runs and a new single-season program record of 56 RBI.

Oneacre's RBI count ranks fifth in the Big Ten and 27th nationally.

title: Report: Penn State men's basketball transfer Eli Rice commits to Middle Tennessee
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State transfer Eli Rice is set to reunite with former teammate Jahvin Carter and return to his home state.

Rice has committed to Middle Tennessee, according to Sam Kayser of League Ready.

NEWS: Penn State transfer Eli Rice has committed to Middle Tennessee, source told @LeagueRDY.The 6-foot-8 wing played the last two seasons at Penn State, averaging 6.7PPG and 1.7RPG this season. Shot 39% from 3. Repped by @jshaugn_23 of @SHASportsAgency. https://t.co/l7IdGheUlB pic.twitter.com/PqJCbFpJgj— Sam Kayser (@KayserHoops) April 23, 2026

Rice and Carter were teammates on the 2024-25 Penn State team. Carter transferred to the Blue Raiders last offseason and averaged 10.8 points per game this past season.

Rice, who's a native of Gallatin, Tennessee, will move closer to home as his collegiate career continues. Middle Tennessee is his third team in his career as he played for Nebraska prior to joining Penn State.

The 6-foot-8 wing averaged 6.7 points and 1.7 rebounds per game with the Nittany Lions in 2025-26, shooting 39% from 3-point range. His shooting hit a cold streak toward the end of the season, causing him to be benched in the Big Ten Tournament game against Northwestern.

Rice only made one appearance in the 2024-25 season with Penn State due to injury, so he redshirted that year. He has two seasons of eligibility remaining as he joins the Blue Raiders.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's volleyball set to face Charleston for EIVA Semifinal
author: Liana Tiamzon I For the Collegian
description:
Undefeated conference champions Penn State isn’t looking back, with a perfect record behind it. Instead, the Nittany Lions are focused on what comes next as they prepare for the EIVA semifinals, where they face the Charleston Golden Eagles, setting up a high-stakes rematch between familiar opponents and a potential NCAA Tournament berth on the line.

The Nittany Lions enter the postseason with momentum from a consecutive nine-game winning streak against Ohio State, NJIT, Charleston, Sacred Heart and George Mason. They have established themselves as one of the most complete teams in the EIVA. They have shown balanced attacks and a strong defensive presence, led by blockers like Owen Rose, who averaged 1.80 blocks per set and leads the EIVA with 129 blocks on the season.

Sophomore outside hitter Sean Harvey has led the charge offensively, hitting .373 with 3.80 kills per set. He has been a consistent force, recording 20 kills in one match and 18 in the next. Harvey had also been named EIVA’s Player of the Year, solidifying himself as one of the conference's top scoring threats.

Penn State swept Charleston in back-to-back matches earlier this month, controlling nearly every aspect of play. In the first meeting, the Nittany Lions cruised to a 3-0 sweep. They were hitting .338 as a team while holding Charleston to just .062. They followed it up with another straight-set win the following day.

The difference in the net was very clear between Penn State and Charleston.​​ The Nittany Lions outblocked the Golden Eagles 14-4 in the first match, led by Rose’s 10-block performance. The Nittany Lions consistently disrupted Charleston’s offensive rhythm and made the blocks into transition opportunities.

Offensively, Penn State relied on balance rather than a single standout performance. Matthew Luoma led the team with 11 kills and four service aces. Harvey stayed consistent and added 10 kills in the win. That distribution has become a defining characteristic of the Nittany Lions’ attack late in the season.

Penn State’s recent success has been driven by efficient hitting, aggressive serves and a disciplined defensive system that limits its opponents' runs. Charleston, though, presents a different challenge in a postseason setting. The Golden Eagles bring size at the net, especially in the middle, which could test Penn State’s offense again.

While Charleston struggled to find their rhythm on offense in their last Penn State meeting, it’s a win-or-go-home scenario and they have a lot riding on this Thursday's match.

The Golden Eagles' physical presence and ability to generate points at the net could shift momentum quickly if the blue and white allowed extended runs.

Even so, Pavlik expects a competitive matchup regardless of past results.“We’re gonna face a similar team, regardless,” Pavlik said.

Since this game is a single-elimination format, execution becomes even more critical for both Penn State and Charleston, as small lapses can quickly change the outcome. “You’re not worried about what happened in the past,” Pavlik said. “Let’s control what we can control now.”

Penn State has long dominated the series against Charleston, posting a 20-1 record since its first meeting in 2016. The Nittany Lions have been particularly strong on the road, remaining undefeated in matches played at Charleston, but this time the game comes to the Rec Hall.

If Penn State can replicate the efficiency and composure it showed earlier this month, it will be well-positioned to advance and take another step toward an EIVA title.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball to close April with trip to Illinois for penultimate regular season series
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is down to its final six games of the regular season, starting with a trip to Champaign to face Illinois for a three-game set.

Both teams are coming off midweek wins, with the Nittany Lions defeating Saint Francis 12-0 and the Fighting Illini overcoming Indiana State 9-5.

Illinois is 14-33 with a 3-14 conference record, most recently dropping 2-of-3 games to Maryland.

After dropping its series against Ohio State, the blue and white fell to 8-10 in Big Ten play with a 25-17 record overall. Penn State’s trio of games against the Fighting Illini is an important one as the Nittany Lions currently rank 10th in the conference, and are still on the brink of earning a spot in the 12-team Big Ten Tournament field.

The Illinois offense is led by a trio of junior bats: outfielder Skylar Brennan, along with infielders Adisyn Caryl and Eileen Donahue.

Brennan’s eight doubles and .343 batting average lead the team, while Donahue owns a team-high 11 home runs and 34 RBIs. The latter was a big performer for the Fighting Illini in its most recent outing, recording three RBIs and a home run.

Caryl is second on the team in these categories, with a .326 batting average, seven doubles and 29 RBIs.

Penn State’s offense is paced by the .385 batting average of freshman infielder Allison Oneacre, along with junior utility player Brooke Klosowicz’s .370 average.

Against Ohio State, Oneacre set the Penn State single-season RBI record after knocking a two-run home run in the Nittany Lions’ 15-7 Game 2 victory. The freshman stands at 56 RBIs on the season, along with 16 home runs to lead the blue and white.

Klosowicz has the second-best numbers for the offense, with 49 RBIs and 13 home runs.

Oneacre, Klosowicz and the rest of the Penn State bats will face a Fighting Illini pitching staff that owns a 5.72 ERA, the third-highest mark in the conference.

Senior right-hander Karley Yergler has been the most productive arm for Illinois. The Mahomet, Illinois, native leads the staff with a 4.76 ERA, 54 strikeouts and 100 innings pitched.

Freshman left-hander Abby Sabalaskey has also put in the work for the Fighting Illini, owning a 4.81 ERA and 48 strikeouts in 84 1/3 innings pitched.

The Nittany Lions own a 4.20 ERA, placing seventh in the Big Ten. In the blue and white’s Tuesday night performance, its staff tossed 10 strikeouts and allowed just one hit in five innings played.

Junior right-hander McKenna Young’s 11-4 record and 3.35 ERA are the best on the team. Freshman right-hander Mackenzie Duncan leads the rotation with 67 strikeouts, while junior right-hander Bridget Nemeth’s 15 starts and 63 1/3 innings pitched are team-highs.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball set to face Oregon for road series
author: Ava Aguiar | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Nittany Lions are on the road again this weekend as they prepare to travel to Eugene, Oregon, where they will take on the Ducks in a three-game series matchup.

The first game is set for 9:05 p.m. in PK Park, where Penn State will have a chance to boost its ranking ahead of the Big Ten tournament.

In the conference rankings, Oregon currently sits at number four, where Penn State is tied for last, fighting for the number twelve seed to make it into the competition.

The blue and white have won two of their last games, with both being high-scoring matchups and strong offensive performances. They played a tough game against Rutgers on Sunday, winning 14-13, and against Bucknell on Monday, going 9-7.

Oregon’s biggest strengths so far fall into the same category as Penn State, with both teams' strongest qualities lying in their offense and at-bats. Oregon takes the win, having more batters hitting over .300 or close to it, where Penn State sits just below .200.

Key Match-Ups

With both teams relying on their offensive line-ups to put runs on the board, here are a few match-ups to look out for, which will be sure to bring in a good competition.

Starting with the Ducks, senior Drew Smith has been a standout player whose statistics come in close comparison with Nittany Lion Michael Anderson. Both players are highly regarded on their teams, with Smith having 13 home runs compared to Anderson’s 18, and a batting average of .373 coming up just short of Anderson’s .379.

The Ducks also see a strong performer in Ryan Cooney, with a slugging average of .642, coming in close comparison with Bryce Molinaro’s .548. Cooney’s batting average is more closely related and just higher than Anderson’s at .388.

The Nittany Lions will also be sure to rely on their main rotation of Jack Porter, Jayden Davis, and Spencer Barnett for runs, who have seen the most success over the last few weeks for the team, with Davis hitting a homer Monday night that helped lead the team to a win.

On the Mound

The Ducks have the upper hand on the mound this weekend, with extremely low ERA’s of around 3.0 or lower, where Penn State has struggled a bit, with averages ranging from 5-8, but has recently seen improvement throughout their last couple games. Pitchers like Frankie Sanchez, Colin Fitzgerald, Ethan Bauerschmidt and Kyle Emmons will most likely start out the rotations.

Penn State will look to improve its record to 13-26 and prove itself as a team ahead of this weekend's series.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's basketball earns commitment from NC State guard Devyn Quigley
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State's squad continues growing in numbers.

The Lady Lions earned a commitment from former NC State guard Devyn Quigley on Thursday, marking the third player on Tanisha Wright's 2026-27 roster.

Quigley is coming off her second season with the Wolfpack, where she averaged 4.3 points and 2.4 rebounds.

The Manchester, New Jersey, native has played in 62 games with NC State, earning six starts.

Quigley put up a career performance against Wake Forest in February, scoring 14 points with 4-for-6 shooting from deep. A month later in the second round of the NCAA Tournament, she scored 11 points against No. 2-seeded Michigan.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: 4 storylines to watch at Blue-White Practice
author: The Daily Collegian football staff
description:
The annual Blue-White Game is almost here, but it’s going to look a bit different than usual — it will instead be Blue-White Practice with stretching, a walk-through, individual drills and some 7-on-7 and 11-on-11.

But regardless of the name or structure, Saturday marks fans’ first look at Penn State under Matt Campbell. There will be plenty of new faces on the Beaver Stadium grass alongside Campbell, some of which the Happy Valley faithful will soon know very well.

Besides the new faces, there’s many other things to look out for. Here are four storylines we’re keeping an eye on at Blue-White Practice this weekend.

Andrew Deal: What does the defensive line truly look like?

Penn State has clearly made it a point to not only add size on the defensive line through bigger players, but also bulk up the other defensive linemen who’ve returned.

The previous defensive lines have been noticeably undersized, prioritizing speed rather than power. However, that’s allowed some of the bigger teams to push around the line, and bully its way in the run game.

The Nittany Lions added size, including transfer defensive tackles Armstrong Nnodim, Siale Taupaki, Dallas Vakalahi and Keanu Williams, all of which are at least 19 pounds over the 300 mark. Does the added size immediately mean more power? That won’t be known until the push at the line can really be noticed.

The Blue-White Practice will have some drives, involving offense versus defense like a normal game. This is where the defensive line could make an impact, and could be where they truly give an answer to the question on power.

The four previously mentioned are likely impact players at the position, and while it won’t be flashy, it could be a sign for how the run defense will perform in the upcoming season, helping fill gaps better to allow the pass rush on the outside to succeed more.

Despite the defenses getting a lot of hype over the past few years, the sack numbers haven’t hit that mark. Last year, Penn State ranked No. 33 in the country in sacks per game, but with the added size on the interior, the outside pass rusher could find more success, even in the Blue-White Practice.

Andrew Mercer: Who will emerge as the go-to running back?

Matt Campbell identified three running backs, each with a different background prior to arriving in Happy Valley, that are in the battle to play similar roles to how Nick Singleton and Kaytron Allen performed during their four years at Penn State.

However, what we don’t know is who’ll be the go-to when times get tough.

Senior Carson Hansen appears to have the upper edge, having followed Campbell out of Iowa State where he was the starter, putting up 950 rushing yards for six touchdowns last year.

Meanwhile, junior James Peoples transferred from Ohio State where he was the third-string running back. In 2025, he continued to play that third-string role behind Bo Jackson and CJ Donaldson, accumulating 352 yards on 61 carries.

The third option is returning Nittany Lion Quinton Martin Jr. The redshirt sophomore saw the field just once last year in the Pinstripe Bowl, where he balled out for 103 rushing yards.

The three of them should get ample time to prove to the coaching staff why they deserve to get more carries come Week 1. With both Peoples and Martin Jr. being younger than Hansen, fans will likely keep an eye on that 1-on-1 battle for the future of the running back room.

“That's going to be great competition all the way through fall camp,” Campbell said. “All three have shown really great things.”

Lexie Linderman: How will the wide receivers look?

It’s no secret Penn State’s wide receivers have struggled in recent years, but with a new head coach, wide receivers coach and a fresh group of wideouts, things could be looking up for the receiving corps.

But expectations were high for the receivers last season and they disappointed, so it’s too early to truly tell if the wide receivers will actually make an impact. Still, the Blue-White Practice will give us the first true look at some of the new wideouts.

It’s unclear how much Brett Eskildsen and Chase Sowell will participate, as they’ve been limited with injuries all spring, but returnee Koby Howard and Iowa State transfers Karon Brookins and Zay Robinson have impressed throughout practice.

All three should get plenty of burn on Saturday regardless of how much Eskildsen and Sowell practice, and they’ll likely leave fans excited about the present and future state of Kashif Moore’s group.

Eyes will especially be on Howard after the buzz surrounding him and his performance as a true freshman last season. Offensive coordinator Taylor Mouser and quarterback Rocco Becht have had plenty of praise for him this spring.

“Koby, he's a guy that's work ethic challenges everybody in the room, not in a bad way, but he's a guy that never leaves the jugs machine,” Mouser said on April 9. “He's a guy that's around the facility all the time. He's a guy that's asking me questions, and when you prepare like that, it forces everyone else to prepare, and you stand out.”

Noah Aberegg: Will Ryan Clanton’s offensive line truly be “Dog like?”

While Penn State’s offensive line led the way for record-breaking seasons by running backs Nick Singleton and Kaytron Allen, things will be a little different this year.

Both running backs are projected to be drafted into the NFL this weekend, while four of Penn State’s starting offensive linemen from last year look to join them.

A few familiar faces will be back for another year with the Nittany Lions, including Anthony Donkoh, Dominic Rulli and Cooper Cousins. But besides them, the rest of the line would be starting their first game with Penn State.

New offensive line coach Ryan Clanton preaches a dog-like mentality for his linemen, and Penn State is getting its first taste of that.

“You got to want to be violent,” Clanton said in March. “You got to want to run through somebody’s face. And that’s a big part about playing o-line.”

As a whole, Penn State’s offensive line is 10 pounds heavier than last year, already playing to that stronger mentality. But it’s unclear if there will truly be a difference on the field.

With a new group of younger and less-experienced backs playing behind the line, Saturday will be the first true test of how well this year’s team can create gaps and opportunities for big plays.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Film review | Miami (Ohio) transfer Brant Byers brings high-octane shooting to Penn State basketball
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Mike Rhoades and Penn State were active in the transfer portal last week, landing commitments from four players as the staff works to improve from last season’s 12-20 record.

Possibly the most noteworthy of the bunch was Miami (Ohio) transfer Brant Byers, who projects to be the starter at the three for the Nittany Lions next season. His style of play fits what the team was looking for in transfer portal additions this offseason, largely due to his 3-point shooting prowess.

Here’s what to know about Byers, who averaged 14.2 points and 4.1 rebounds per game, as he’s set to join Penn State.

Deep 3-point shooting range

The Nittany Lions made it a priority to add more perimeter threats on offense, and Byers fits that perfectly. He shot 39.2% from 3-point range on 5.2 attempts per game this past season, and 58.9% of the field goals he attempted were 3-pointers.

Byers has a deep range as well, typically knocking down shots from far behind the 3-point line.

Byers shot 39.2% on 3-pointers this past season, taking 5.2 attempts per game.He has a deep range, knocking down shots far behind the 3-point line pic.twitter.com/rg1xBJlat9— Collegian Basketball (@CollegianHoops) April 22, 2026

Byers has a quick release and a consistent shooting stroke, making him a catch-and-shoot threat. He also utilizes spot-up shooting as he can immediately make a defense pay after receiving a pass on the perimeter.

Byers doesn’t hesitate when he has an open look from 3-point range, making him a constant catch-and-shoot threat pic.twitter.com/Bt3QcyVRwE— Collegian Basketball (@CollegianHoops) April 22, 2026

Byers’ 3-point shooting will be a welcomed trait to the Nittany Lions, who could’ve used that type of production this past year. He’ll stretch the floor for Penn State next season and should provide reliable production beyond the arc.

Attacking close outs

With Byers often getting the ball on the perimeter, there’s usually a defender attempting to make up ground and contest a potential 3-point shot. If Byers doesn’t take the deep shot, he can drive into the paint for a layup.

With the chance of Byers shooting a three when he gets the ball beyond the arc, he can attack close outs and drive into the paint for a layup pic.twitter.com/yPQIyQszYZ— Collegian Basketball (@CollegianHoops) April 22, 2026

Byers doesn’t hesitate when deciding between a 3-point shot or moving closer to the basket, which sets him up well to usually get an open opportunity around the rim.

It’s another wrinkle to Byers’ game that makes a defense have to defend more than just a 3-point shot pic.twitter.com/q1yZ4rEU9L— Collegian Basketball (@CollegianHoops) April 22, 2026

Byers won’t be used in isolation or one-on-ones much while on offense, as that’s not his style of play. But his ability to drive in after faking a 3-point shot adds another wrinkle to his game, so he’s more than just a sharpshooter.

Interior scoring

With Byers playing off-ball on offense, he has the freedom to move around and get open in the interior as well for buckets. Byers shot 60.2% on 2-pointers during the 2025-26 season, getting looks in the mid-range or in the paint off of a pass.

Byers also showcases some off-ball movement to get open for a layup, such as this back-door cut: pic.twitter.com/9nhlPJfqGO— Collegian Basketball (@CollegianHoops) April 22, 2026

He can finish well near the rim, sometimes laying in baskets off balance.

Byers shot 60.2% on 2-pointers and can finish well at the rim pic.twitter.com/Un69ETIqvr— Collegian Basketball (@CollegianHoops) April 22, 2026

Byers is going to make more impacts beyond the arc, but he can still use off-ball movement or back-door cuts to get a quick two instead of a three.

Defense

As for his defense, Byers defends in the interior, guarding opposing teams’ power forward with his 6-foot-8, 200-pound frame. During Miami’s run in the NCAA Tournament, Byers got beat at times, recording four fouls in the loss to Tennessee.

Byers shot 60.2% on 2-pointers and can finish well at the rim pic.twitter.com/Un69ETIqvr— Collegian Basketball (@CollegianHoops) April 22, 2026

Byers still exhibits a ton of effort on the defensive end, trying to always keep a good position on whoever he’s guarding. He only averaged 0.6 steals and 0.4 blocks per game in 2025-26, but his positioning helped him average 3.4 defensive rebounds per game.

Byers’ defensive numbers don’t jump off the page, but he does get a block or a steal every once in a while pic.twitter.com/oA3MQvavjI— Collegian Basketball (@CollegianHoops) April 22, 2026

Again, where Byers is going to be a game changer for Penn State is in 3-point shooting. While his defense might have some flaws, it’s good enough to stay on the floor so he could find more usage on offense.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State football draft profile | OL Vega Ioane’s high character, power key to success at NFL
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
After Penn State’s fifth straight loss, this one coming at the hands of Ohio State, Vega Ioane didn’t have his head down or sulk, instead, he was seen pushing an equipment cart up a ramp, helping a staff member.

That type of character and his on-field play is why Ioane will likely hear his name at some point on Thursday of the NFL draft.

After starting in five games his redshirt freshman season, Ioane earned the starting left guard role as a redshirt sophomore. His blocking was a reason for the Nittany Lions’ running success en route to a College Football Playoff semifinal appearance.

Ioane started every game his last two seasons, and was often used as a gadget player in the versatile playbook of former offensive coordinator Andy Kotelnicki, sometimes being used as a blocker in motion to lay the boom on an edge rusher.

“My four years here, that biggest adversity I've ever faced, you know, I was 2000 miles away from home, took a chance on being here growing up as a man,” Ioane said at pro day. “It's exactly what Penn State did for me. Came to campus as a kid, left as a man.”

Metrics

Height: 6-foot-4

Weight: 326 pounds

Vertical jump: 31.5 inches

Broad jump: 8-foot-8

Projections

Projected round drafted: Round 1

Player comparison: Tyler Booker

Scouting report

Ioane stands out on the offensive line. In a unit that was pretty strong the past two years, he was the anchor, the mauler and the protector, no matter what the play call was.

His power has allowed him to fend off some of the top linemen in the Big Ten, protecting Drew Allar from interior threats. However, given his size, his speed isn’t necessarily there despite his usage as the gadget lineman.

What he lacks in speed he makes up in footwork, though. Ioane’s flexibility is also top-tier, and he was never reliant on Kotelnicki’s scheme, which makes him an even better prospect, as any team could feel comfortable taking Ioane.

Ioane’s rise into the first round came after dominant 2024-25 seasons as a pass blocker, not allowing a single sack despite over 800 pass-blocking snaps. He also didn’t pick up a single holding flag.

Part of the reason his pass-blocking is so effective is his sturdy feet. Defenders can’t get past him because he doesn’t waver and anchors himself into the grass, using his strength to keep pass rushers at bay.

Although Ioane only started games at left guard, he’s taken snaps at every position on the offensive line and has been training this draft process so he’s prepared to play center if necessary. Ioane also had a stint at right guard last spring before JB Nelson transferred.

Best team fit: Pittsburgh Steelers

The Pittsburgh Steelers have a glaring hole at left guard. After losing Isaac Seumalo in free agency to the Arizona Cardinals, Spencer Anderson is the presumed starter.

Anderson played a good role as an extra offensive lineman, and has held his own when needing to fill in, but question marks arise if he’d be able to last and hold it down as a consistent lineman on the field.

Ioane would immediately slot in as the starting left guard and likely thrive next to center Zach Frazier, adding to the young but strong offensive line that the Steelers have built through the draft.

The big question is if Ioane will be available at pick No. 21. Recent mock drafts have him going as high as No. 14 to the Baltimore Ravens, so if the Steelers want the top interior offensive lineman in the NFL draft, they’ll likely have to move up to take him, or hope he somehow falls into their lap.

MORE FOOTBALL CONTENT

title: Senior Column | Ugly roads lead to beautiful destinations
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
Slightly over a month into my freshman year, I got sick. It felt like a cold, so I treated it as such. But when Central Michigan came to town on Sept. 24, 2022, I had someone tell me I looked green in Beaver Stadium.

The next day, I was diagnosed with pneumonia at University Health Services.

As all good moms do, mine immediately drove to State College and got an Airbnb to try to nurse me back to health. I applaud your efforts, Mom, but I was a wreck, violently coughing, had a spiking fever and consistently went from having chills to freakishly sweating.

That Friday, we moved to a different Airbnb, one that had been purchased months prior as my younger brother, Will, and my dad visited for the Northwestern football game. They went to it because I convinced them I was fine, but in reality, I was hacking up a lung and felt like death.

Not sure why I told them to go, or why they listened to me, but we can move on. Either way, it’s one hell of a way for the family to visit you at college for the first time.

On Sunday, I moved into Mount Nittany Medical Center, where I said goodbye to my mom and Will while having several needles injecting me with IVs and fluids. My oxygen was low, and my white blood cells were crazily spiking.

After six days of no sleep, taking insane amounts of medications and my dad running around State College getting me food so I didn’t have to eat the hospital stuff, I made it out. After one more sleep in a different Airbnb, I returned home to Briarcliff Manor, New York, to recover for a few days.

Then I returned to school, my lungs permanently damaged and still coughing a ton. From November to winter break (with a case of the flu the week before Thanksgiving, where I went home again), my days were spent going to class and coming back to Geary Hall, sleeping and slamming antibiotics.

By April, I was feeling somewhat like myself. I made some friends and covered a few baseball games for The Daily Collegian after not doing anything in the fall on the women’s soccer beat. I returned home for the summer and somehow passed all my classes.

I had a great summer, spending time with family and friends from home. I was still operating on tons of medication and had countless doctor appointments, but I felt normalish.

One afternoon, I was going on a walk around my neighborhood with my Mom, reflecting on my freshman year. I admitted that it sucked, mainly pointing at my health as the reason. She asked if I had thought about transferring. I immediately said no.

Truth be told, I had thought about it a lot my freshman year. I thought about it when I returned that fall, in the spring and that summer.

The reason was I didn’t know what my social life would look like. I had some friends, but despite hanging out with them, part of me felt like I was the kid clinging on. When I was sick, people were finding roommates for the following year, so I was forced to go with a random on-campus roommate. Everyone I hung out with would live together off-campus, and that worried me.

I never said any of this until a few days before I left for sophomore year, where instead of talking it out with my family, I broke down crying at the dinner table, saying I didn’t want to leave my friends at home.

Eventually, I made my way to school, scared out of my mind. My random roommate, Colin Baverso, turned out great, and while we didn’t hang out after that year, it’s always nice running into him. My friends that I was scared weren’t actually my friends are some of the closest friends I have to this day. I also made new friends.

My sophomore year rocked. My grades went up, my social life took a step forward and my health was turning a corner.

That joy was largely due to the Collegian. I didn’t really do anything my freshman year, so I sometimes wonder how I made staff again, but I’m glad I did. I decided to become more invested in the paper, returning to the sports staff covering women’s soccer.

I didn’t have a passion for sports journalism before that fall, but I most certainly found it. I loved that season covering women’s soccer, writing everything I could, earning reporter of the semester for sports staff and switching my major from economics to digital and print journalism.

But I wanted more. Covering women’s soccer and becoming an update merchant for other beats wasn’t enough. I joined women’s basketball and men’s lacrosse, doing anything I could to write and contribute to the Collegian. The office became a home. The people became a family.

By the end of the year, I earned reporter of the semester again, and was honored to be selected for the illustrious football staff, ready to grow on the beat that so many talented reporters come through. After two practices and the Blue-White Game, I was hooked as I headed home for the summer.

Another walk with my mom that summer brought up another question, where she asked me if I wanted to do football since my social life took off, with a big part of that being tailgating in the fall. I deflected, but like the last walk, it was something I thought about a lot.

In reality, I’d been doing a lot of thinking, but not just about that.

While my life had taken off on paper, that entire sophomore year, I didn’t bring up my life-altering freshman year. Why would I? It sucked. Nobody at school was there for it. Nobody saw and understood what I went through. So, I keep it to myself.

I never really talked about it with my friends from school or home. I never really talked about it with my family either.

That summer, my parents could tell something was wrong with me, though I didn’t know what was wrong with me. One afternoon, and I couldn’t tell you how it happened, I let it all out.

I cried again, but I also unleashed every single bottled-up emotion and thing that needed to be said over the last two years. I couldn’t tell you the specifics of what was said, but what I learned was that I had mental health issues going on.

I hate saying “mental health issues” which ended up being part of the issue. I never really wanted to talk about my past, because to me, it seemed like the soft thing to do. Stupid as hell, I know.

Before I left for junior year, I talked with my family a lot and had some professional conversations. It took a bit, with a handful of emotional moments in between, but I entered junior year in a good headspace, having cleared a lot out mentally.

Once I settled into University Gateway Apt. 406, my home for the next two years alongside AJ Piazza and Luke Sheluck, I talked about what went down that summer. They listened and were understanding. Shocker. That was all I needed, to be honest.

A week later, I found myself in West Virginia to kick off covering football, leaving at 5 a.m. and not getting back to school till midnight due to a three-hour delay of thunder and lightning.

Football was everything I wanted and then some. I went to West Virginia, Wisconsin, Purdue, Minnesota and Indianapolis for the Big Ten Championship, Arizona for the Fiesta Bowl and Florida for the Orange Bowl in a season that Penn State was a drive away from playing for the national championship. Those were the best months of my life alongside the best psufootblog in Collegian history with Joel Haas, Lexie Linderman and Avery Hill.

And another shocker — during and after the season, I hung out with my non-Collegian friends despite not tailgating.

Junior year was better than sophomore year. I briefly had a run on the photo staff for some reason. I turned 21, where I was introduced to Doggies, the greatest place on earth.

Senior year came around in the blink of an eye, where Will Horstman joined the football crew with Joel exhausting eligibility. Joel got employed and still covers Penn State, so he’s just Unc now and essentially never left.

The season was different, as a national championship caliber team turned into James Franklin being fired, a 7-6 record and the hiring of Matt Campbell. I went to UCLA, Iowa, Ohio State, Michigan State and Rutgers. The season capped off with the Pinstripe Bowl at Yankee Stadium, where I’ve been going to Yankees’ games since I was a little kid as a fan, so a pretty cool way to cover my last football game.

I churned out articles, picked up a wrestling editor position somewhere in between, working with a bunch of hooligans in AJ, Andrew Deal and Evan Smith. A lethal group to say the least, but an elite one at that, and an even more elite group chat.

I continued to write, edit, podcast, occasionally do a newsletter and successfully avoid making pages, but my time is up as my responsibilities are not covering Penn State sports, but filling out job applications on TeamWork Online.

I want to thank sports journalism. It gave me a passion for something I didn’t know I had. I’ve always loved sports, but I didn’t know I loved writing this much. My English grades have always been good, but I didn’t know this was where writing could take me.

I love storytelling. I love talking to athletes and coaches. I love going on camera and talking about Penn State football. I love working so hard on a story that, in the end, I’m proud of it.

I got a byline writing for the Associated Press covering the Super Bowl this year. I got to travel all over the country to see college campuses, cover a College Football Playoff run and be in four NFL stadiums. I got to cover a coach being fired and a coach being hired. I wrote for the Pittsburgh Post-Gazette. I wrote for the Cape Cod Baseball League for the championship-winning Bourne Braves.

I gained over 3,700 followers on X, and had someone come up to me at Champs while I was double-fisting dirty sprites to tell me how much he loved reading my work and following me.

I asked questions to Franklin, Campbell, Drew Allar, Abdul Carter, Nick Singleton, Tyler Warren, Terry Smith, Sam Darnold, Steffon Diggs, Cooper Kupp, Jaxon Smith-Njigba, Rece Davis, Desmond Howard, Jarrod Saltalamachia, Gavin McKenna, Cael Sanderson and more.

I watched Jaxson Dart and Cam Skattebo eat wings in front of me. I watched Justin Jefferson and Michael Vick play Madden together. Drake Maye, Christian McCaffrey, Ashton Jeanty, Fernando Mendoza, Pat McAfee, Nick Saban, Stephen A. Smith, Deion Sanders, George Kittle, Curt Cignetti, George Kittle, Rick Pitino and Tom Izzo have been right in front of me.

Journalism is a horrendous industry. Sports journalism is a horrendous industry. I’m aware of that, hence why I’m keeping my professional options open. I think my resume, paired with close to 1,200 bylines with the Collegian, on top of being pretty good at what I do, makes me qualified for whatever professional job, but that’s not how the world works.

If this is my last byline, I wouldn’t want it to be for any other outlet. The Collegian has poured so much into me with all these opportunities and awesome people I’ve met. Back when I was unsure of how my sophomore year would look, the Collegian was there for me when nothing else was.

I hope I left it in a better place. Nothing brings me more joy than helping out others on the sports staff, hearing how excited people are about their stories and coverage and hanging out at the office where I’ve spent more time than my own apartment. People truly don’t realize what a special place it is. For anyone who has ever talked shit about the Collegian, respectfully, fuck you. You don’t know what you have.

Thank you to those that the Collegian has brought me — Evan, Will, Myles Hannak, Chase Mitchell, Zach Karpf, Kaitlyn Murphy, Kate Hildebrand, Adam Waxman, Sam Woloson, Joe Tuman, Olivia Estright, Seth Engle, Liam Wichser, Daniel Mader, Tyler Millen and more.

Thank you to the various versions of the “Where is Lyle” groupchat. Thank you for being my friends after I stupidly thought at one point that none of you really were. I no longer look worried.

Thank you to my hometown friends. Though the group chat isn’t active unless we’re all home, I love you all to death.

Andrew, you’re the man. It’s been awesome getting to know you and watching you grow the past two years. I’m fired up for you to be on football and know you’ll thrive. There aren’t a lot of people who truly pour so much into the Collegian, but you are most certainly one of them, and I am forever grateful for that.

Greg, it’s been a pleasure becoming your friend despite our first conversations over text. I thought you were a grade below me. You’re one of the nicest people I’ve ever met and you never have a bad thing to say about anybody. Though I’ll never forget the time a certain reporter fooled you into saying they were working on a feature the whole semester, just for them to then quit. I enjoyed your reaction to that. You’re one of a few people who I can truly talk sports journalism with, but also have even deeper conversations about Panda Express.

Joel, last year you said you didn’t write enough about me, so I’ll take this opportunity to do so about you. Thank you for being my first sports and football editor. I look up to you a lot in this sports journalism thing, and I appreciate all the guidance. If I’m not on the beat next year, you will now become my primary source of Penn State football content, and know you’ll continue to kill it in covering Penn State, moving heavy weights and Clash Royale. Thanks for letting me sit in your seat this year, but also for being a role model.

Lexie, despite you initially hating me without ever saying a word to me, and becoming a bit to your friends, you turned out alright. I hope I did too. I couldn’t ask for a better person to cover games with the last two years and make fun of for quite literally anything, but I’ll pick on you for not liking food and being gullible for the sake of time. You’re an incredible person and can’t wait for you to shine as editor, and for everyone to see what I know you’re capable of.

Avery, it’s been one hell of a ride. From you editing my horrendous baseball stories in the summer, to the Bryce Jordan Center, to Beaver Stadium, to covering the Super Bowl, it’s been a journey. You’re as talented as they come, but also an awesome person who knows as much ball as me. Being on the same beat the last three years has been a blessing, and hopefully one day we will work together as professionals in an area that has no string pins in its bowling alleys.

To the Other Team, you know who you are. You know what they say. Why not us? It was always. I’ll add you both are some of my best friends to add a wholesome component here.

AJ and Luke, thank you for being goated roommates for the last two years. The Kegway will never be the same once we’re done with it. Thank you for putting up with my horrendous coughing when it strikes. You both are incredible people and some of my best friends. Thank you for appreciating my love for halal cart — the only acceptable halal cart in State College.

Nana, grandma and grandpa Bill, thank you for always being in my corner. It means the world to have you all as a support system, and I wouldn’t have it any other way. Please continue to root for Penn State over Illinois for the foreseeable future, despite me graduating and Will still being in school.

Mom and dad, thank you for everything. You guys have been my rocks for as long as I’ve been alive and I’m forever grateful. The amount you have both done for me, and put up with has been a lot, especially over the last couple of years, but I’m incredibly appreciative. I can’t really put into words the impact you’ve both had on my life, but hopefully this will suffice.

Will, I still can’t believe you picked Illinois over Penn State, which means I don’t have a place to stay when I visit State College for the next couple of years. That being said, I’m looking forward to finally getting to campus after I get this photo tour done. Thank you for being my best friend and being my favorite person since you were born. Fired up to see what you accomplish and what’s down the road for us.

It’s time to wrap this up. The name of this column is “Ugly roads lead to beautiful destinations.” I felt like it was pretty fitting.

Making it to this enjoyable part in my life wasn’t pretty. Not the sickness. Not the doubt. Not the sleepless nights where I questioned everything. Most of the road to get to where I am was ugly.

But those moments led me here. To the people and experiences I wouldn’t trade for anything.

So if this is the end of my time at Penn State and the Collegian, I’ll leave it with this:

Ugly roads lead to beautiful destinations. This one was worth every step.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Film review | Scouting new Penn State women’s basketball commits Ava Black, Kamrah Banks
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After Tanisha Wright was hired as the next coach at Penn State on March 19, all 10 of the Lady Lions' returners entered the transfer portal.

Starting from scratch, Wright earned her first two commitments this week in 4-star incoming freshman Kamrah Banks and former Vanderbilt freshman Ava Black.

While both are younger pieces to Penn State’s squad, they offer a solid starting point in a rebuilding team with big moments early in their careers. Here’s a look at what the two new guards have to offer in Happy Valley.

Kamrah Banks

After committing to UCF due to a strong bond with the coaching staff, Banks reopened her recruitment after head coach Sytia Messer was fired in March, and ultimately landed as the first player at Penn State.

Throughout her time at Crispus Attucks High School, Banks showcased her abilities as a high-volume scorer. The 5-foot-9 guard left Indianapolis as the school’s all-time leading scorer, also holding a record for the most points in a game, breaking NBA Hall of Famer Oscar Robertson’s previous record.

Against North Central High School in December, Banks put on a dominant 30-point performance with four rebounds, three assists and two steals.

Despite being a guard, Banks showed no hesitation driving to the basket, scoring against pressure from the opposing center.

Against North Central High School, Banks takes the ball from the top of the key to the basket, bouncing off a defender for the score pic.twitter.com/In5ZjiWoJW— Noah Aberegg (@aberegg_noah) April 22, 2026

While she only hit two 3-pointers against the Panthers, she tallied 43 made triples her senior year, with an even-better 48% mark from deep in her junior year.

Banks shows no hesitation pulling up from deep, sinking one of her two 3-pointers against North Central pic.twitter.com/VGydfDQA6q— Noah Aberegg (@aberegg_noah) April 22, 2026

While she was known throughout her high school career as a high scorer, Banks was also efficient, dishing the ball, averaging 2.7 assists per game during the 2025-26 season.

Banks’ physicality likely would’ve aided her in scoring many of these opportunities. However, her IQ and vision led to an easier score with an assist to her teammate.

Banks shows strong court vision against the Panthers, finding an open teammate for an easy score pic.twitter.com/kvSGY3bWyi— Noah Aberegg (@aberegg_noah) April 22, 2026

Ava Black

Despite playing just 10.2 minutes per game, Black had several big games throughout her first year.

Her biggest game came at the biggest moment, when No. 2-seed Vanderbilt hosted No. 15-seed High Point in the first round of the NCAA Tournament. Black had a career day, scoring 12 points with three rebounds, an assist and a steal.

While Black plays as a bigger guard, standing at 5-foot-11, she still displayed the skillset of any guard, including being a 3-point threat.

Vanderbilt transfer Ava Black found herself open on the 3-point line for her second straight basket, sinking the triple to extend Vanderbilt’s dominant lead pic.twitter.com/OHUefex4jQ— Noah Aberegg (@aberegg_noah) April 22, 2026

However, one of the Auburn, New Hampshire, native’s best qualities comes in her playmaking, as she helps kickstart the Commodore offense.

After being fed the ball at the top of the key, Black dished it back to her teammate and found the open crease to the basket, receiving the ball and scoring on the way.

Black’s playmaking was on display with a give-and-go opportunity, finding herself running toward the basket for the wide-open score pic.twitter.com/bvifd82xMd— Noah Aberegg (@aberegg_noah) April 22, 2026

Black’s skill sets extend beyond her offensive scoring, as she also displayed success with on-ball defense.

The newest Lady Lion played defense at the top of the key, stealing the ball and making her way to the other end of the court for a fast-break layup.

Black’s success showed up on defense too, with fast-break steal and score to extend her career day in the NCAA Tournament pic.twitter.com/alwMeMT9oB— Noah Aberegg (@aberegg_noah) April 22, 2026

title: ‘I’ve never seen anyone like her’ | Freshman phenom Rocquette Allen wows with Penn State women's lacrosse
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After committing to Syracuse in 2024, Rocquette Allen flipped to Penn State, following coach Kayla Treanor to Happy Valley.

With the Nittany Lions, Allen put on one of the most impressive freshman campaigns in the nation, earning numerous awards during her standout season.

“She’s just a phenomenal kid,” Treanor told The Daily Collegian. “She’s just so incredibly hardworking and super competitive. She’s just a really special kid.”

While she’s currently one of the best freshman players in the country, Allen didn’t grow up playing lacrosse. Instead, she began as a swimmer and finally picked up a lacrosse stick in middle school, traveling the country as she progressed in her early career.

“Honestly, in my opinion, swimming isn’t as fun anymore, just swimming back and forth,” Allen told the Collegian. “There’s no creativity compared to lacrosse. So my Dad bought me a real stick and I absolutely fell in love with it.”

After playing for her middle school team, Allen joined coach Aliyah Weant at Mountain Vista High School and traveled across the country during the offseason to practice and play with SkyWalkers lacrosse in Maryland.

Since Allen lived in Colorado, lacrosse was a smaller sport and didn’t have a lot of competition. With the SkyWalkers, she got to play in the capital of lacrosse.

“The opportunity for her to go play out on the East Coast was huge because the competition she saw there was a shocker,” Weant told the Collegian. “Her progression over the summer was significant.”

With the SkyWalkers, Weant noticed the effect of playing alongside other high-level athletes had on Allen, as the blooming prospect returned for each school season with a greater skillset.

At the same time, Allen began to be recruited by several schools, including Syracuse, by Treanor.

“I remember the first time watching her play and just thinking, ‘Oh my God, I love this kid,’” Treanor said. “She was electric to watch. She was a true crease attacker.”

As Allen continued to play with both her high school team and with the SkyWalkers, she showcased her work ethic and continued to set herself apart from her teammates.

“Roc is consistent,” Weant said. “Every time she shows up to a game, you know you’re going to get the best out of her. She’s consistent and her stick skills are unmatched. I’ve never seen anyone like her, ever in my career.”

During her junior year, she committed to Syracuse, a perennial national championship contender for women’s lacrosse. When Treanor made the move to Happy Valley, so did Allen.

“I’m just a huge fan of hers,” Treanor said. “I loved her in the recruiting process, so I was just thrilled that she came with me to Penn State.”

Arriving to Happy Valley in the fall, Allen quickly returned to the hard work and hustle she had put on display throughout her high school days.

“I’m not thinking about the future, it’s just how I can help my team out today,” Allen said. “I can always do better, I can always work harder, be better at serving everyone.”

As the season started, Allen was one of three Nittany Lions named as Big Ten Players to Watch and eventually started in all 17 games for the blue and white.

After scoring two of Penn State’s 10 goals in the season opener, the Lonetree, Colorado, native recorded her first of many five-goal games against UMass four days later.

“(Her performance) wasn’t surprising to me at all,” Weant said. “She’s just going to be back at the practice field working for the next one.”

Allen experienced her second five-goal game against Bucknell, followed by a lower-scoring game where she only scored two goals against Pitt. However, her second goal came at the most crucial time, defeating the Panthers in overtime.

“The thing that stood out to me most was her finishing ability behind the net,” Treanor said. “No angle is still a great angle for Rocquette Allen, and she’s one of the first players that I’ve seen play that I’m like, 'Ooh, that was similar to how I played.’”

The freshman star continued to battle through the season, earning her first Big Ten Freshman of the Week honor after scoring a goal with three assists against Drexel.

That was her first of many awards throughout the season as she continued to stuff the stat sheet. Midway through the season, Allen led the Nittany Lions in goals scored, making SportsCenter-caliber highlight plays in the process.

“I put the work in outside of practice,” Allen said. “Knowing that I’m working outside of practice and practicing those things, it’s all going to translate. Perfect practice makes perfect.”

Allen finished her freshman regular season with her sixth hat trick of the year, tallying 41 goals with 22 assists in Year 1. The young star also recorded her sixth Big Ten Freshman of the Week honors, capping off her season with a unanimous All-Big Ten Freshman Team selection.

“This year alone she’s had a lot of success,” Treanor said. “Knowing Roc, it’s only going to continue to climb. Just when you think that’s it for her, there’s always going to be something.”

While Allen wants to succeed and win a national championship during her four years at Penn State, her focus is on her day-to-day actions and being the best she can be.

“All I care about is God, winning and just being a better person,” Allen said. “I am very grateful for (awards) and everything I did to get those, but at the end of the day, there’s more to give and more to prove.”
